Please describe your research career to date
I am a qualitative primary care researcher with interests in medical overuse, risk communication, and the role of
evidence and values in medical care. I have an academic background (MSc/MPhil) spanning medical sociology,
philosophy, and public health. My research is particularly concerned with the social meanings that patients and
clinicians ascribe to diagnosis. I have published my research in leading journals, including Sociology of Health and
Illness and Journal of Medical Ethics, and have presented widely nationally and internationally. I have held NIHR
academic fellowships throughout clinical training: first as an Academic Foundation doctor and then as an academic
GP trainee.
Overdiagnosis research (2019-present)
My MPhil (see below) sparked my interest in how clinicians negotiate risk and uncertainty and how they
communicate these issues to patients. I published a single-author paper in Journal of Medical Ethics on how to
explain the pitfalls of s

secarb__001_b.text:
creening to patients (top 5% of all Altmetric-scored outputs). I was invited to present this
work at Preventing Overdiagnosis 2022.
I then completed a Diploma in Philosophy of Medicine, exploring in my dissertation how clinicians balance risk
when diagnosing patients as dying. This was awarded the Sydney Selwyn prize for the best Diploma dissertation. I
was invited to give a public lecture on my research and published it as a single-author paper in Journal of
Evaluation in Clinical Practice (2022). I presented this work at Preventing Overdiagnosis 2023.
I belong to the Preventing Overdiagnosis Early Career Researchers group and am part of the pre-conference
organising committee for the 2025 conference.
NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship, King's College London (2021-Aug 2025)
I have been a Research Fellow on Dr Andy Guise’s UKRI-funded Social Responses to Stigma project since
beginning my academic placement in 2023. This ethnographic study examines how stigma and discrimination
arise within the health and care services used by people who are homeless in south London. My work includes
ethnographic observation in day centres and collaborative analysis of fieldnotes and interviews. I le

secarb__001_c.text:
ad a research
strand exploring intersections between stigma, homelessness, and health self-management.
Outputs include:
 Presentation at the RCGP Conference 2024.
 First-author paper in preparation (for submission to Sociology of Health and Illness).
 Co-author on a quantitative survey of homeless people’s stigma experiences, (under review, BMJ Open).
 Co-author of a forthcoming submission on drug use stigma (International Journal of Drug Policy).
In 2022, I collaborated with researchers at Charité University Berlin and Medical University of Vienna on a project
to improve Type 2 diabetes prevention in women with gestational diabetes. We secured a £10,000 European
University Hospitals Alliance grant and convened workshops in London, Berlin and Vienna. I am first author of our
interdisciplinary commentary paper (provisionally accepted January 2025, Journal of Midwifery and Women’s
Health).
Other activities:
 PGCert in Applied Research Methods (KCL) (completion June 2025). Awarded Distinction in all completed
modules.
 Advisory board member (2022-2024) for PREPARED, an interventional primary care study to improve
pregnancy outcomes in women with diabetes.
 Invited peer editor f

secarb__001_d.text:
or Professor David Misselbrook’s textbook, Thinking About Medicine: An Introduction to
the Philosophy of Medicine.
 Peer reviewer for Social Science and Medicine, British Journal of General Practice, Journal of Medical
Ethics, BMC Medical Ethics, Digital Health, and Information, Communication & Society.
MPhil, University of Cambridge (2019-2020)
My MPhil (First Class with Distinction) combined medical philosophy, sociology and anthropology. My thesis
(Starred Distinction) was a qualitative interview study exploring how women’s experience of pregnancy was
shaped by participating in an online gestational diabetes support community. I published this work as sole author
in Sociology of Health and Illness (2022) and presented it at three international conferences, including the
International Federation of Gynaecology and Obstetrics World Congress (2021).
Previous research experience
Honorary Research Fellow, Queen Mary University of London (2017-2019):
 Led a systematic review of post-partum depression and intimate partner violence in high-income countries
– awarded 1st prize at the British Society for Biopsychosocial Obstetrics and Gynaecology conference
(2018).
 Co-authored a syste

secarb__001_e.text:
matic review and meta-analysis of health service use by people with severe mental
illness, which was published in PLOS Medicine (2021).
 Led the quantitative analysis of the LGBT Foundation’s survey of women who have sex with women
(2019), which was presented at the All-Party Parliamentary Group on Sexual and Reproductive Health.
MSc, London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine:
 Quantitative analysis of the National Survey of Sexual Attitudes and Lifestyles, focusing on birth order and
sexual health outcomes. This work – published as a first-author paper in Sex Education (2018) – attracted
significant media attention, including coverage in the Independent and the Daily Mail.
(https://www.independent.co.uk/news/education/education-news/sex-education-first-born-children-parents-
families-london-school-hygiene-topical-medicine-a8558211.html).
MBBS, Newcastle:
 Co-led a national survey of medical students’ views on abortion education, presented at two international
conferences (2016). I was subsequently invited to the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists
to discuss curriculum recommendations.
Teaching and management
In 2018, I co-designed and delivered a critical appr

secarb__001_f.text:
aisal module for Global Health BSc students at QMUL. I was
also awarded the Foundation School Award for Teaching Excellence in recognition of my mentorship of medical
students applying for Academic Foundation posts. I have held Associate Fellowship of the Higher Education
Academy since 2019.
From 2015-2016, I was Policy and Advocacy Director for Students for Global Health UK, leading a team of policy
analysts and steering four national student research collaborations. As chair of the Cambridge Global Health
Lecture Committee (2019-2020) I coordinated a series of six public lectures with over 200 attendees.
Impact of fellowship
A Doctoral Research Fellowship will develop my skills in complex qualitative methodologies and strengthen my
portfolio of research addressing medical overuse. Following a PhD, I aim to secure an Academic Clinical
Lectureship to conduct further work exploring the social and relational factors influencing decision-making in
primary care, focusing on how to navigate uncertainty and foster shared understanding between patients and
clinicians. I am especially interested in co-producing creative outputs to engage wider audiences in considering
how medical resources

secarb__001_g.text:
can be used wisely. My eventual goal is to become a Professor of Primary Care, using
interdisciplinary qualitative methods to inform excellence in primary care delivery.

Contextual Factors
Please use this section to detail any factors that may have impacted the development of your research career or
outputs from your research career to date. NIHR wants to know about any circumstances so that they may take
them into consideration during the assessment of your application. Contextual factors may include:
● Career breaks due to parental leave, or periods of illness.
● Reduced time spent undertaking research activities (including research outputs), due to disability or to
caring responsibilities. This may include physical or mental health related impairments or conditions, which have
impacted your opportunities to underta

secarb__001_h.text:
ke research activities
● Reduced opportunities for career support e.g., mentorship, and limited opportunities to undertake prior
research and training.
● Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on your research career
Please also use this section to detail any other factors that may have impacted your research career not listed in
the examples provided. The impact on your career to date will be specific to your particular circumstances but
could include such impacts as limited opportunities to obtain grant funding or fewer publications. In general terms,
contextual factors should be significant, and relevant.
NIHR acknowledges that you may be reluctant, or uncomfortable disclosing relevant information that is sensitive.
However, you should bear in mind that we are unable to take into account factors that you do not disclose. Please
be assured that the information provided by you is sensitive and will be treated confidentially and in line with the
General Data and Protection Regulations (GDPR).

secpesor__001_a.text:
Background
Communicating effectively about blood testing is important for ensuring patient safety and involvement in
healthcare. Blood tests are useful, but do not always improve patient care. Sometimes they can cause harm: for
example, borderline test results may worry patients or lead to unnecessary further tests. This is important because
blood tests have become increasingly common: general practice (GP) testing rates have tripled since 2000.
Previous research has shown that patients often feel unsure about why blood tests are done and what results
mean. GP staff do not always clearly explain the reasons for testing and the limitations of blood tests, and patients
report not having enough opportunity to speak to staff about testing. This means that GP staff and patients may
have different expectations and understandings of blood tests. Understanding what happens in consultations
about blood tests is important for addressing the rise in testing and ensuring tests are used wisely.
Aims
This project will explore how blood tests are discussed between patients and GP staff, aiming to improve
communication about the limitations and risks of blood tests. The goal is for patients to be
secpesor__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_b.text:
more involved in
decisions about blood testing and understand better what blood tests mean for their health.
Research design
The project has four parts:
1. Evidence search: I will search and review existing research on how blood tests are discussed in general
practices. This will help to understand what is already known and what is missing.
2. Observation at GP practices: I will spend several months watching conversations about blood tests at two
different general practices. I will make recordings of patient consultations with GP staff (including GPs,
nurses, pharmacists and phlebotomists) to look at how blood tests are discussed.
3. Workshops with GP staff: I will show short recordings of blood test discussions to staff. They will consider
how blood tests are discussed with patients and think about how communication can be improved.
4. Theatre workshops with the public: Professional actors and public contributors will create a short play
based on the research. This will be performed to members of the public as part of “Forum Theatre”
workshops. Workshop attendees will be invited to make changes to the play to improve communication
about blood tests. Public contributors will help t
secpesor__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_c.text:
o develop the play into two tools to improve blood test
communication:
a. A workshop for general practice staff.
b. An online education resource for patients.
Patient and Public Involvement (PPI)
People who have recently had blood tests will be involved throughout the project. Nineteen public contributors with
diverse ages, ethnic backgrounds, and health experiences helped to shape the project over three workshops.
They described many issues with communication about blood tests and felt strongly that this research was
important.
A PPI panel will guide the research to make sure the project responds to the needs and preferences of people
having blood tests. The panel will meet three times a year to review project plans at each stage and provide input
on data analysis. The panel will also write public summaries of the research.
Panel members will be paid for their contributions. They will receive funding for training and attending conferences
to share the research. Two lay people will co-chair a separate Project Advisory Group to steer the project.
Sharing the research
I will share the research with local community groups, patients and GP practice staff through social media, blogs, a
secpesor__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_d.text:
podcast, and a public event. The online resource for patients will be made available on a project website. I will use
the workshop tool created from the play to train GP staff and trainees on better blood test communication.
secpesor__001_d.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secsa__001_a.text:
Background
The use of blood tests in primary care has increased significantly: testing rates have tripled since 2000, and nearly
half of all NHS tests are now conducted in this setting. While valuable, blood tests are often over-ordered, leading
to high costs (£1.8 billion in 2015-16), increased clinician workload, and potential harm to patients. These harms
include anxiety, undue reassurance, and unwarranted further investigations.
Communicating effectively about the limitations, risks, and purpose of blood tests is critical, yet research shows
that blood test communication is often inadequate. Clinicians rarely explain the purpose or limitations of tests,
while patients often report feeling excluded from testing decisions and unsure about the implications of their
results. NHS guidelines for optimising blood testing in primary care focus primarily on test appropriateness, with
little attention to how tests are discussed with patients.
Understanding what happens in consultations about blood tests is important for addressing the rise in testing and
ensuring that blood tests are used wisely. With patients now able to access blood test results online, these
communication issues warra

secsa__001_b.text:
nt urgent attention.
Aims
This research will use an ethnographic approach to explore how and whether blood test uncertainty, overuse and
overdiagnosis are discussed in primary care settings. It will employ participatory methods to identify how these
issues could be better communicated.
Research questions
1. How is communication about blood testing in primary care settings framed in the literature?
2. How, where and when do issues like uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis emerge in primary care
interactions about blood testing?
3. What challenges do clinicians face in communicating about blood tests, and how can video-reflexive
ethnography help clinicians to identify solutions to these challenges?
4. How can participatory performance methods engage patients and clinicians to identify more effective ways
of discussing issues with blood tests such as uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis?
Methods
A multi-method study across four work packages (WPs):
 WP1: Meta-ethnography synthesising existing qualitative literature on primary care blood test
communication.
 WP2: Detailed ethnographic observation in two general practices with contrasting patient populations,
focusing on consultat

secsa__001_c.text:
ions where blood tests are discussed.
 WP3: Video-reflexive ethnography workshops with primary care staff to identify complex challenges
around blood testing communication as well as opportunities for change.
 WP4: Forum Theatre workshops with members of the public and primary care staff to develop strategies
for improving blood test communication. The findings will inform a co-produced workshop (for clinicians)
and an online resource (for patients) to support better blood testing discussions.
Impact
This participatory research project will highlight the primary care consultation as a key site of opportunity for
prudent blood testing. It will engage primary care staff and the public in co-producing resources to encourage more
effective communication about the limitations of blood tests.
Dissemination
The workshop resource will be shared with primary care staff and GP trainees locally, nationally and
internationally. A version of the workshop will be made available as a publicly-accessible resource for patients.
Research findings will be published in academic journals, presented at conferences, and shared through blogs and
podcasts.

secdrp__001_a.text:
1. What is the problem?
Effective communication about blood tests is important for shared decision-making and patient safety.1,2 Primary
care blood testing has increased substantially, but patients are often not fully informed about the purpose, risks, or
limitations of blood tests. Consultations about blood tests are key moments for involving patients in their care and
ensuring the appropriate use of primary care resources. This project aims to improve how primary care staff and
patients communicate about blood testing.
Nearly half of all NHS laboratory tests are requested in primary care,3 with testing rates increasing threefold
between 2000-2016.4 Research suggests that blood tests are often overused: the 2021 Why Test study of primary
care blood tests found that up to a quarter were unnecessary.5 Overuse entails avoidable cost and added
workload for clinical staff 6,7 and can also lead to overdiagnosis: the identification of abnormalities that would
never have caused harm. Borderline results, for example, may prompt anxiety or unwarranted follow-up
investigations.8 Conversely, normal results can provide false reassurance, delaying accurate diagnosis or
diverting attention away

secdrp__001_b.text:
from patients’ presenting symptoms.9
Patients need to understand the limitations and risks of blood tests so they can make informed decisions about
testing. However, these issues are not always well-explained. GPs recognise that tests are not always useful and
may increase rather than reduce clinical uncertainty,10 but often initiate blood tests without discussing alternatives
or explaining the limitations of testing.11 This means that patients may have different expectations of blood tests.
Many patients struggle to understand their results and do not have enough say in decisions about whether to
have tests,12 limiting their opportunities to be involved in their care.
NHS England has produced guidelines for optimising blood testing in primary care but these focus only on the
tests.13
clinical appropriateness of blood They do not address how clinicians should communicate to patients
which tests are necessary, nor how to effectively explain the pitfalls of testing. While guidelines are a useful aid for
clinical decision-making, they cannot replace the need to explain the rationale behind testing decisions and
explore patients’ expectations of tests.
Understanding what happens in cli

secdrp__001_c.text:
nical consultations where blood tests are discussed is crucial to addressing the
rise in testing and ensuring that tests are used wisely. Research focusing on these ‘on the ground’ interactions is
missing and is vital to inform improvements in clinical practice. By observing real-world interactions between
clinicians and patients, this project aims to identify ways to enhance blood test communication and thereby
support more prudent, patient-centred testing practices.
2. Why is this research important?
This research is the first to use ethnographic methods to explore how blood tests are discussed in GP settings. I
will directly observe communication between primary care staff and patients, providing important insights into how
communication can encourage or hinder appropriate blood test use in the GP setting. I will use innovative
participatory methods with primary care staff and the public to identify practical solutions to improve blood test
communication.
Primary care staff are generally highly trusted, but some patients do not feel supported to make decisions about
their health.14 Improving how primary care clinicians communicate about the limitations of blood tests is crucial

secdrp__001_d.text:
to
help patients understand the implications of tests and their results. This is also important for patient safety: for
example, ensuring that patients understand that normal test results may not rule out serious illness.
The project responds to concerns about unsustainable workloads in general practice. A survey by the Royal
College of General Practitioners (RCGP) found that three-quarters of GPs feel patient safety is being
compromised by excessive workloads,15 while the British Medical Association has warned of ‘a growing crisis’ in
care.16 results,4
primary With the average GP spending an estimated 90-120 minutes per workday reviewing test
strategies to reduce unnecessary investigations may go some way towards mitigating the clinical and
administrative burden of testing.
This research is timely. The NHS Long Term Plan stipulates that all patients must have access to their online
records – including test results – by the end of 2024,17 and online access has been mandated in all English GP
practices since 2023.18 This has significant implications for blood test communication. Online access can create
unintended consequences, including increased workload and patient anxiety about

secdrp__001_e.text:
test results.19,20 This project
will offer in-depth insight into how digital access shapes blood test communication.
Wise use of blood testing is important for healthcare sustainability. Primary care clinicians request 45% of NHS
blood tests, costing approximately £1.8bn in 2015-16.4 Unnecessary blood tests can yield avoidable follow-up
costs, including hospital visits and investigations.21 Supporting clinicians and patients to have more candid
conversations about the limitations of blood tests may help address medical overuse in the NHS, enabling cost
savings and reducing healthcare's carbon footprint.22
3. Review of existing evidence
Primary care test requests have increased by 8.7% per year on average since 2000.4 Testing is expensive4 and
contributes significantly to GP workload.6 While blood tests can provide important clinical information, many tests
requested are of little diagnostic value and do not improve patient care. The Why Test study, which reviewed
blood test results for 2572 patients across 57 UK GP practices, found that only 6.2% of tests led to a new or
confirmed diagnosis, and 48.8% did not result in patient reassurance or any change in clinical management.5
Unne

secdrp__001_f.text:
cessary and escalating blood testing exposes patients to the risk of overdiagnosis, wherein patients are
harmed by further investigations or treatments without receiving any benefit.7 The potential harms of blood tests
may be overlooked compared to more invasive diagnostic procedures, but the sheer volume of blood testing
means that these cumulative harms warrant careful consideration. Borderline results are common – in one study
of GP blood tests, nearly three-quarters of patients had at least one test result outside the reference
range.5 These results can lead to unnecessary anxiety, particularly if patients access them via online apps.20
False-positive or borderline results can also lead to ‘investigation momentum’: further testing or invasive
procedures such as biopsies or irradiating scans.8 Alternatively, normal test results in a patient presenting with
symptoms may provide undue reassurance, delay diagnosis, or distract from lifestyle changes.9
Despite their limitations, patients have high expectations of blood tests as diagnostic tools23,24 and may
underestimate their potential harms.25 Patients often request blood tests as a ‘check-up’ 23 despite limited
evidence for the v

secdrp__001_g.text:
alue of many tests in asymptomatic individuals.26,27 This means that patients and primary care
staff may have different ideas about the purpose and value of testing. In a UK-based qualitative study by Watson
et al., patients felt that blood tests would provide answers to and solutions for their symptoms; by contrast, GPs
were less optimistic about blood tests, viewing them as a way to rule out serious problems rather than pin down
specific diagnoses.12
Miscommunication between clinicians and patients appears to be an important mediator of medical overuse.
Research into antibiotic prescribing practices in primary care, for example, suggests that clinicians often mistake
expressions of concern about symptoms as requests for antibiotics.16 Similar patterns may underlie overuse of
blood tests: research shows that clinicians may initiate testing to satisfy or affirm patients regardless of whether
there has been an explicit patient request.17 Where patients do request blood tests, clinicians may acquiesce –
even if they feel blood tests are unlikely to be of use – in order to avoid longer discussions about the merits of
testing.9
Although there is a wide body of research using directly-o

secdrp__001_h.text:
bserved data to examine communication in primary care
consultations, 28–33 only one UK-based study focuses on blood test discussions: a secondary content analysis of
27 audio-recordings from an archive of GP consultations. This showed that shared decision-making was rarely
evident in blood test discussions.11 While the study quantified various aspects of poor communication – for
example, that patients were given the opportunity to ask questions about blood test results only 13% of the time –
nobody has used naturalistic data to explore the nuances of blood test communication in-depth.
Further research is needed to understand how blood test discussions might be improved so that:
1. Clinicians are better equipped to explain to patients why blood tests may (or may not) be necessary.
2. Patients understand what blood tests mean for their ongoing care.
3. Both clinicians and patients can better appreciate and convey the potential benefits and limitations of
blood testing.
4. Aims/research questions
Aims
This research will use an ethnographic approach to explore how and whether blood test uncertainty, overuse and
overdiagnosis are discussed in primary care settings. It will employ partic

secdrp__001_i.text:
ipatory methods to explore how these
issues could be better communicated to encourage judicious use of blood testing.
Research questions
1. How is communication about blood testing in primary care settings framed in the literature?
2. How, where and when do issues like uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis emerge in primary care
interactions about blood testing?
3. What challenges do clinicians face in communicating about blood tests, and how can video-reflexive
ethnography help clinicians to identify solutions to these challenges?
4. How can participatory performance methods engage patients and clinicians to identify more effective
ways of discussing issues with blood tests such as uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis?
The research questions will be addressed in four sequential work packages, each with specific objectives as
outlined below.
5. Project plan
Work Package (WP) 1: Synthesising existing qualitative literature on primary care blood testing
discussions.
Objectives
1. Identify when, where and how blood tests are discussed in primary care.
2. Explore how patient and clinician perspectives on and experiences of blood testing are framed in the
existing literature.
3. Deve

secdrp__001_j.text:
lop new conceptual understandings of blood test communication in primary care settings to inform
later work packages.
Methods
I will conduct a meta-ethnography of qualitative literature on communication about blood tests in primary care.
Meta-ethnography is a form of interpretive qualitative synthesis involving the re-interpretation of primary data and
themes in published research.34 Unlike other aggregative qualitative synthesis approaches, meta-ethnography
aims to develop new theories about the topic under study.35 This work will therefore build on an existing mixed-
communication.36
methods systematic review of blood test In a preliminary scoping review I identified
approximately 20 papers that provide the level of textual detail necessary to support meta-ethnographic synthesis.
I will follow Noblit and Hare’s seven-step process for meta-ethnography to generate theoretical interpretations
which reflect the qualitative synthesis.37
Search strategy
I will systematically search medical, social science and public health databases, using appropriate search terms
and operator functions with the support of QMUL library staff. I will also search grey literature via thesis
repositories,

secdrp__001_k.text:
Google Scholar, citation searches, and reference list reviews. To ensure relevance to contemporary
practice, I will limit my research to papers published in English from 2000 to the present, a period which has seen
a significant rise in blood testing.4
Studies will be imported into Mendeley Reference Manager. After removing duplicates I will screen the titles and
abstracts of the remaining papers against SPIDER criteria:38
 Sample: staff members working in primary care; patients accessing these services.
 Phenomenon of Interest: blood testing.
 Design: interviews, focus groups, case studies, ethnography.
 Evaluation: motivations, thoughts, beliefs, understandings.
 Research type: qualitative.
I will screen the full texts of selected papers. A random sample of 50% of these papers will be double-screened by
a second reviewer (e.g. Professor Swinglehurst). We will meet to resolve any disagreements.
Data extraction
Study characteristics will be imported into a shared spreadsheet. The second reviewer and I will assess the
quality of each study using Joanna Briggs Institute qualitative research criteria.39 Data will be extracted over
multiple readings, capturing themes, first-order

secdrp__001_l.text:
constructs (participant quotes) and second-order constructs (the
study authors’ interpretations).
Data analysis/synthesis
I will work with a second reviewer in the analytical stages of the meta-ethnography and will also consult with
qualitative researchers from the QMUL APOLLO team to ensure rigour. Findings will be translated between
studies by mapping concepts from one study to another, using a constant comparative method to identify where
study findings converge or diverge. Shared themes and concepts from across studies will be integrated into third-
order constructs (new interpretations) to develop a ‘line of argument’.34
al.40
I will present my findings using the eMERGe reporting framework developed by France et These findings will
guide my ethnographic approach and analysis in later work packages.
WP2: Understanding blood testing practices and interactions in the primary care setting.
Objectives
To use ethnographic methods to explore in-depth:
1. How decisions to do blood tests are discussed between patients and GP clinical staff.
2. How and whether uncertainty about blood testing decisions or results is communicated.
3. How and whether issues of blood test overuse or overdia

secdrp__001_m.text:
gnosis are discussed.
4. The approaches used by GP staff to convey and discuss blood test results.
5. Patients’ experiences of using digital tools to access blood test results.
6. The role of language and non-verbal communication in blood test interactions.
Methods
Building on findings from the meta-ethnography, I will conduct an ethnographic study of blood testing practices
and communication in the GP setting. Ethnography – involving immersion in a particular setting through
observation, interviews,fieldnotes and reflexive diaries – is increasingly recognised as a valuable approach for
primary care research.6,41–44
Ethnography helps uncover beliefs, behaviours and motivations which may be taken for granted or viewed as
unremarkable by patients and clinicians.45,46 By capturing meaningful details that people might not remember or
mention in interview studies,47 it can reveal the complexity of everyday interactions. This makes it particularly
useful for studying safety and quality in healthcare.46 Ethnographic approaches have been successfully used in
research on polypharmacy to develop new understandings of how medical overuse emerges and is sustained
through social norms, practice

secdrp__001_n.text:
s, and interactions.45,48,49
Sampling
Participants in PPI workshops felt it was important to explore blood testing practices in diverse patient groups and
across different geographical settings. I will therefore purposively select two contrasting GP practices: an inner-
city practice with a young, multi-ethnic population, and a suburban practice with an older population. I will use
General Practice Fingertips data to identify potential research sites which will be recruited with the support of the
NIHR Research Delivery Network.50
Data collection
I will undertake observation in each site 2-3 times per week over 4-6 months (approximately 200 hours in total).
This schedule, informed by similar studies,6,43,48 balances the need for deep immersion in the field with the
practical constraints of being a lone researcher.
Data collection will include:
1. Observation of professional practices
I will observe clinical consultations, reception/administrative areas, interprofessional meetings,
o
and Patient Participant Groups. Only half of primary care blood tests are ordered by GPs,5 so I
will also observe pharmacists, nurses and phlebotomists to ensure the dataset reflects the
diversity of pr

secdrp__001_o.text:
imary care blood testing interactions. I will make detailed fieldnotes during and after
observations.
2. Ethnographic interviews with patients and GP staff
o I will conduct informal ad-hoc interviews with patients and GP staff, which will be audio-recorded
(with consent) or included in fieldnotes.
o To explore digital communication about blood tests, I will conduct ‘think-aloud’ interviews, inviting
patients and staff to describe their thoughts as they use digital resources like the NHS app.6,51
3. Gathering written communication relating to blood testing
o I will gather and analyse relevant written materials, e.g. consultation notes, screen captures from
patient apps.
4. Recording consultations
o I will record approximately 45 consultations/’think-aloud’ interviews covering key stages of the
blood testing process, e.g.:
a. discussing/ordering tests;
b. receiving/filing blood test results;
c. communicating results to patients.
Dataset diversity
I will ensure that a range of primary care clinicians are represented in the recordings, and will include
consultations with patients of different ages, sexes and ethnicities, as well as those with multimorbidity and mental
illness.
I will a

secdrp__001_p.text:
lso record consultations involving interpreters and patient advocates/carers. I have made provision in the
budget for patient information and consent forms to be translated into the three most common patient languages
(besides English) in each research site.
Consent
I will seek consent for recording from patients and clinicians either prospectively (for face-to-face consultations) or
retrospectively (e.g. if seeking to access audio consultation data from practice telephone systems). Participants in
PPI workshops were surprised that many GP practices automatically recorded telephone consultations and felt
patients needed to be told this before being approached for consent. I will consider this in the consent procedure,
with input from the PPI panel and research sites. Clinicians and patients will be able to withdraw their consent for
recording during the consultation or request that the recorded data be destroyed at any point afterwards.
I discuss the feasibility of recording further in Section 9.
WP3: Exploring and addressing professional concerns around blood test communication.
Objectives
1. Elicit the perspectives of primary care staff on the challenges of communicating about bl

secdrp__001_q.text:
ood tests,
especially when dealing with uncertainty or potential overdiagnosis.
2. Provide a safe and supportive environment for clinicians to trial and refine alternative communication
strategies.
Methods
Video-reflexive ethnography (VRE) is an innovative participatory research method which involves filming people in
situ doing their usual work, then showing the footage back to them in facilitated workshops.52 Participants in VRE
workshops watching these recordings see familiar practices from a fresh perspective, stimulating discussions
about what they may have taken for granted and where they might see possibilities for change. This approach
means that those delivering care are actively involved in the process of data analysis and quality improvement.
VRE has been successfully used in various healthcare settings, including maternity care, 53, acute medical units,54
and dementia services.55 My supervisors (Professor Swinglehurst/Dr Fudge) have also used VRE to explore
primary care conversations about polypharmacy – another aspect of medical overuse.56
Workshops will be conducted according to the four key principles of VRE:52
1. Exnovation: acknowledging the complexity of existing

secdrp__001_r.text:
practices and encouraging innovation with existing
resources.
2. Collaboration: valuing participants’ expertise and supporting a process of co-creation.
3. Reflexivity: encouraging participants to re-evaluate their assumptions, beliefs and practices.
4. Care: creating a supportive environment for participants and researchers.
Data collection
I will run 6 one-hour workshops (3 per site), with 6-10 primary care staff in each. I will schedule these in practice
training time to maximise engagement. Workshops will include a mix of staff including GPs, nurses, pharmacists,
phlebotomists, and practice managers.
I will show selected short recordings from WP2 to prompt interprofessional discussions about blood testing
conversations in primary care. The chosen clips will focus on ‘critical moments’ in blood test consultations which I
will select based on iterative analysis and the findings of the meta-ethnography.
The workshops will provide an opportunity for primary care staff to identify good practices and rehearse a range of
strategies to support improved communication with patients. Workshop discussions will be audio-recorded and
transcribed for analysis.
WP4: Using participatory perform

secdrp__001_s.text:
ance methods to explore how to improve blood test communication
Objectives
1. Co-create an interpretation of study findings with the PPI panel and professional actors.
2. Bring together members of the public and primary care staff to develop strategies to improve blood test
communication.
3. Co-produce resources, including a workshop and public engagement materials, to help improve
communication about the limitations of blood tests.
Methods
Forum Theatre is a participatory performance method allowing participants to enact, critique and transform real-
world scenarios. It is one of the tools for social change described in Augusto Boal’s Theatre of the Oppressed,
developed in the 1960s/1970s with marginalised communities in Brazil.57
In a Forum Theatre workshop, actors perform a scene involving conflict or misunderstanding while audience
members (“spect-actors”) observe the social dynamics which emerge.58 A facilitator then invites spect-actors to
suggest or enact alternative actions to change the scene, improving the dramatised interaction through iterative
improvisation.58
Forum Theatre is gaining traction in research exploring how interpersonal interactions can be improved in
heal

secdrp__001_t.text:
thcare.59–61 It is particularly effective for highlighting how power dynamics and inequalities can impact upon
communication. Like VRE, it provides a space for participants to see situations from alternative perspectives and
develop new practices collaboratively.
Co-production process
Part 1
A day-long workshop with the PPI panel and actors from Improbable, an improvisation theatre company. Using
selected research materials (e.g. ethnographic fieldnotes, VRE workshop excerpts), workshop participants will co-
design a short play about blood test discussions.
Part 2
Two Forum Theatre workshops, each with approximately 20 participants, including the public and primary care
staff. Public participants will be recruited in collaboration with Social Action for Health, a charity working closely
with East London communities affected by health inequalities. Led by Improbable, these workshops will explore
how to improve blood test discussions through audience suggestions and interventions.
Part 3
Another day-long workshop with the PPI panel and Improbable actors. A videographer will guide the recording of
short scenes based on the play and Forum Theatre workshops. The PPI panel will help deve

secdrp__001_u.text:
lop these video clips
into two resources to disseminate study findings and support improved blood test communication: a workshop for
primary care clinicians and an online resource for patients (see Section 6).
Analysis
This study will generate a rich dataset, including fieldnotes, audio/video-recorded consultations, VRE workshop
transcripts, and reflexive diaries. I will take an interpretive approach to analysis, combining insights from
ethnographic observation with close study of how language is used in situ by patients and GP staff.62 This
linguistic ethnography orientation offers distinctive insights into the interpersonal dynamics involved in working
practices and how these shape and are shaped by institutional norms. Widely used in education research,
linguistic ethnography is increasingly recognised as a valuable methodology for understanding the role of
language in medical interactions, including in primary care. 41,49,63
My analysis will be grounded in relevant social theory, underpinned by an understanding of diagnosis as a social
phenomenon as well as a clinical tool.64 I will draw upon work by scholars of diagnosis such as Annemarie
Jutel64,65 and Charles Rosenberg,66 as

secdrp__001_v.text:
well as Deborah Lupton’s work on the sociology of risk.67
Data will be managed in NVivo 15. I will use CLAN software to transcribe recorded consultations, using
Conversation Analysis conventions to capture fine-grained interactional details such as pauses and overlaps.68
6. Dissemination, outputs and anticipated impact
Clinician/patient resources based on Forum Theatre workshops
Workshop for clinicians
 The workshop will use the recorded scenes from WP4 as prompts for attendees to consider how blood
tests are discussed and how this could be improved.
 I will deliver the workshop in teaching sessions for local GP practices and VTS schemes, seeking early
feedback to refine the workshops and demonstrate impact.
 I will run the workshop at GP conferences to ensure the resource reaches clinicians nationally and
internationally.
 I will share the workshop through academic GP networks (see Collaborations) and with GP VTS
Programme Directors.
Online resource for patients
 Hosted on the project webpage, this online resource will feature the recorded clips of blood test
communication developed in WP4. It will include written guidance to help patients think about the purpose
and possibl

secdrp__001_w.text:
e outcomes of blood tests and consider the questions they want to ask primary care clinicians.
 The resource will be promoted via social media (e.g. Bluesky/LinkedIn), flyers in community groups/GP
practices, and Patient Participant Group mailing lists.
 Key messages will be also be disseminated to patients via leaflets, newsletters, and GP waiting room
displays.
I will apply for postdoctoral funding to further develop and evaluate these resources.
Dissemination of study findings
To patients/the public:
Study findings will be shared with the public via:
 A project webpage, featuring blogs and the PPI panel’s summaries of the research plan and findings
 Project news updates via Bluesky and LinkedIn.
 An episode of the QMUL Wolfson Institute podcast, Thinking In Between.
 A stall at QMUL’s Festival of Communities, an annual public engagement event which has showcased
QMUL research to over 25,000 attendees since 2016.
To academics/clinicians:
 Publication of at least four peer-reviewed open-access papers in target journals:
o Meta- ethnography (Sociology of Health and Illness)
o Linguistic ethnography (Social Science and Medicine)
o Video-reflexive ethnography (BMJ Quality and

secdrp__001_x.text:
Safety)
Forum Theatre (BMJ Evidence-Based Medicine)
o
 National conference presentations, e.g. Society for Academic Primary Care, Royal College of General
Practitioners, Health Services Research UK.
 International conference presentations, e.g. Preventing Overdiagnosis (a multidisciplinary conference
well-attended by GPs), WONCA (World Organization of Family Doctors).
 Presentations in clinical/academic networks, e.g. RCGP Optimal Testing, University of Bristol Centre for
Academic Primary Care.
To all stakeholders:
 Supported by the QMUL Centre for Public Engagement, I will host a public dissemination event for local
clinicians, community groups and the public to share the findings and discuss how best to implement the
WP4 resources.
Anticipated impact
This research will:
1. Encourage discussions between patients and clinicians about the challenges of blood testing and how
they can be overcome.
2. Foreground primary care consultations as key opportunities for the safe and prudent use of blood testing.
3. Develop a ‘good practice’ approach to discussing blood tests in primary care, co-created with patients.
Key impacts:
For patients: They will better understand the limits of blo

secdrp__001_y.text:
od tests, feel more confident in making shared decisions
about testing, and have a clearer grasp of the implications of their results.
For primary care staff: They will gain confidence in explaining the risks and limitations of blood tests, improving
their ability to discuss testing decisions and results with patients.
For the NHS: Better communication and shared decision-making could improve patient safety and reduce
unnecessary testing. This will reduce blood testing costs, ease administrative workloads, and reduce the costs
and carbon footprint associated with repeat/follow-up investigations.
7. Project management
I will oversee the project’s progress, budget and outputs with support from my supervisors and the QMUL finance
team. I have uploaded a detailed research timetable.
Progress will be monitored through:
Supervision
Fortnightly meetings with Professor Swinglehurst.

 Monthly meetings with Dr Fudge.
 Monthly meetings with Dr Watson (online, with in-person meetings in Bristol twice-yearly).
 Full supervisory team meetings three times a year, with a yearly face-to-face meeting in London.
 Email support and feedback on written work from all supervisors as required.
Proje

secdrp__001_z.text:
ct Advisory Group (PAG)
The PAG will meet online twice a year to assess and guide study progress. PAG members will also provide
mentorship on an ad-hoc basis. The PAG will be co-chaired by lay members Steven Oliver and Cathy Mullins,
who attended pre-proposal PPI workshops. Both have significant public involvement experience, including
chairing meetings, and will receive funding for relevant training.
The other PAG members are:
 Professor Natalie Armstrong (Executive Dean of the School of Health and Medical Sciences, City St
George’s)
Medical sociologist with expertise in applied qualitative research and overdiagnosis.
o
 Dr Julian Treadwell (GP & NIHR Academic Clinical Lecturer, University of Bristol)
Lead of GP Evidence, a co-produced information resource for GPs on treatment benefits/harms.
o
 Dr Margaret McCartney (GP & Senior Clinical Lecturer in General Practice, University of St Andrews)
Internationally-recognised advocate for evidence-based medicine, with extensive broadcasting
o
experience.
 Professor Stephen John (Hatton Lecturer in the Philosophy of Public Health, University of Cambridge)
o Medical philosopher with interests in risk communication and early cancer det

secdrp__001_{.text:
ection.
 Dr Jessica Drinkwater (GP & NIHR Academic Clinical Lecturer, University of Manchester)
o Academic GP with experience of participatory and applied theatre methods.
Formal reviews
As per QMUL regulations, I will have yearly reviews to assess progress and identify additional support/training
needs.
8. Ethics
I will obtain ethical approval from QMUL and the NHS Health Research Authority before starting any data
collection involving participants. Key ethical considerations include:
Ethnographic observation and recording
I will get written consent from all GP practice staff for observation. My presence will be respectful and unobtrusive
and I will leave a space immediately if asked. GP staff will seek a patient’s explicit consent for me to observe their
care, ideally without me present (e.g. when collecting patients from the waiting room).69 I will clearly explain my
dual role as a researcher and practising GP.
Video-reflexive ethnography workshops
Staff may feel uncomfortable watching or hearing comments about their consultations. I will mitigate this by
ensuring that staff are aware of the purpose of the workshops: i.e., to collectively identify new ways of working,
rather th

secdrp__001_|.text:
an critiquing each other’s practice. I will work with Professor Swinglehurst and Dr Fudge to plan these
sessions.
Confidentiality
This project involves video/audio-recordings of clinical consultations. Ensuring the confidentiality and security of
data is thus essential; this was also a high priority for PPI participants. All research data will be securely stored in
line with QMUL’s data management policy, the Data Protection Act 2018, and Research Ethics Committee
requirements. Recordings will be encrypted, securely stored, and deleted from recording devices after transfer.
9. Success criteria
I will measure the success of my PhD according to the completion of the milestones described in the attached
Gantt chart. Key indicators include:
1. Ethical approval secured by end of year 1;
2. Meta-ethnographic synthesis submitted for publication by 1st quarter of year 2;
3. Ethnographic data collection/VRE workshops complete by 3rd quarter of year 2;
4. Co-produced resources for patients/clinicians finalised by 3rd quarter of year 3;
Risks and mitigation
1. Delays in obtaining ethical approval: I will apply for ethical approval promptly upon starting the fellowship.
I will be supported by

secdrp__001_}.text:
my supervisors to prepare a high-quality application to reduce the chance of
rejection.
2. Difficulties completing the meta-ethnography: I have done a rapid scoping search which confirms there is
a suitable body of relevant literature. I will attend two training courses and will work with a QMUL librarian
to prepare the search strategy. Members of the APOLLO team with experience in meta-ethnography will
be able to offer methodological support.
3. Difficulty gaining consent to record consultations: Research shows high rates of patient consent to video-
recording of GP consultations for research purposes.70 Participants in the pre-proposal PPI workshops
also felt that recording consultations would be acceptable. I will seek the PPI panel’s guidance on
recruitment materials including consent forms. I will draw upon my supervisors’ experience of obtaining
consent from clinicians to record consultations56 and will use the period of ethnographic observation to
build trust with GP staff.
4. Challenges in the applied theatre workshops: Forum Theatre may be unfamiliar to participants and they
might feel unsure about the aims or process of the workshops. This risk will be mitigated by the
Im

secdrp__001_~.text:
probable actors’ expertise in improvised participatory theatre. I will be supported by my mentor, Dr
Drinkwater, who has experience of using applied theatre methods.

secppi__001_a.text:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
To develop this proposal, I held three workshops with 19 members of the public who had recently had general
practice blood tests. Participants were from diverse ethnic backgrounds, including Black British, South Asian and
Chinese, and ranged in age from 20s-70s. Several had long-term conditions and/or identified as disabled, and two
participants were neurodivergent.
In the first workshop I asked about participants’ experiences of blood testing, which helped shape my research
questions. In later workshops, we discussed the proposed research: the topic, aims and objectives, methods, and
plans for sharing findings. Participants felt strongly that this research was important. They highlighted
communication difficulties throughout the blood testing process:
 “I don’t feel the GP discusses the decision to do a blood test… they just say you need one.”
 “There’s a lot of difficulty in understanding the results without having to go onto Google.”
They also described concerns about blood test uncertainty or overuse:
 “It’s confusing getting a borderline result - the doctor says

secppi__001_b.text:
we don’t worry when the test is just out of range,
but there’s no time for them to properly explain.”
 “The GP says you haven’t had that blood test in a while and they order it whether you need it or not.”
Participants liked the idea of using applied theatre methods and felt that a play would be an accessible way of
sharing the research. Based on feedback from these workshops, I made the following changes to the project:
 I will observe Patient Participation Groups (PPGs) as part of the ethnography and share my findings in
PPG meetings to involve existing patient networks in the research.
 Participants wanted the workshop resource for GPs to be part of GP training, so I will run the workshop for
local GP training programmes.
 I decided not to include GPs in the workshop session to develop the play, as participants felt that PPI
contributors would be able to participate more freely without staff present.
Two workshop participants co-wrote the plain English summary and provided feedback on the PPI plans. They will
co-chair the Project Advisory Group.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research,

secppi__001_c.text:
including any training and support provided
I will assemble a Patient and Public Involvement (PPI) panel to guide the project, made up of people who have
had a blood test in primary care in the past year. I will ensure panel members are diverse in age, ethnicity and
gender and have a range of health experiences.
I will meet the panel three times a year to discuss study progress and troubleshoot any issues which come up. All
panel members will be paid for their time and travel expenses in line with NIHR guidance. I will also offer payments
for childcare/caring expenses. The research budget includes funding for panel members to access training and
present the research at conferences.
I will seek the panel’s guidance on recruiting research sites and obtaining consent for recordings. I will also gather
their feedback on early findings from observations. This will help me choose recordings to show in workshops with
primary care staff.
The PPI panel will work with a theatre company to develop a play based on the research findings. This will used in
theatre workshops with members of the public to find ways to improve blood test communication. I will recruit
participants for the workshops

secppi__001_d.text:
in collaboration with Social Action for Health, a local charity with extensive
experience of working with East London communities who are not well-represented in research.
Together with the panel and theatre company, I will create short video clips based on the workshops. The panel
will help me to adapt these into a training workshop (for clinical staff) and a public-facing online resource (for
patients), both aimed at improving communication about blood tests. The panel will also develop summaries of the
research plan and findings to post on a project webpage. At the end of the project, the PPI panel will help to host a
public event to share our findings with members of the community and local primary care staff.
Two public contributors helped to develop this proposal and will co-chair a Project Advisory Group which includes
my supervisors and four external academics/GPs. This group will provide scrutiny, guidance and oversight
throughout the project.
If it is considered not appropriate and meaningful to actively involve patients/service users, carers and the
public in your proposed research, please justify why
n/a

sectdars__001_a.text:
Proposed training and development programme
The QMUL Doctoral College and the Wolfson Institute of Population Health offer doctoral development
programmes with courses covering project management, personal effectiveness, and research governance. I will
attend induction events, yearly cohort days, and research skills seminars. As outlined below and in the attached
training timetable, I have planned further training to allow me to successfully complete the PhD and continue my
development as a clinical researcher.
Research skills
Qualitative methodology:
 Advanced Qualitative Research Methods (8 weeks part-time, University of Oxford, £3175): Masters
module focusing on more complex aspects of qualitative research such as reflexivity, bias, and advanced
data collection methods. This will underpin the qualitative methods training I received in my MSc and
MPhil.
Meta-ethnography (WP1):
To refresh my skills in systematic review and develop my understanding of qualitative synthesis, I will take the
following courses:
 Literature Searching for Systematic Reviews (half-day, QMUL): course on literature searching in
preparation for the meta-ethnography database searches.
 Overview of Qualita
sectdars__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_b.text:
tive Review and Synthesis Methods (1 day, University of Bristol): course to aid me in
conducting an interpretive qualitative synthesis.
Ethnography and video-reflexive ethnography (WPs2&3):
Building on my previous experience of ethnographic research, I will take the following external courses:
 Ethnography and Ethnographic Methods (5 days, Essex Summer School in Social Science Data Analysis,
£1,600): module to provide a thorough introduction to the theory and practice of ethnographic research
and set me up for ethnographic observation in GP practices.
 Summer school module on Linguistic Ethnography (5 days, Ghent University, £250): an introduction to key
concepts in linguistic ethnography. This will support me in the close analysis of recorded data in WP2,
building on the Conversation Analysis course (University of Oxford) I completed in 2024.
Creative co-production (WP4):
I will be mentored by Dr Jessica Drinkwater, a member of the Project Advisory Group who has experience of using
forum theatre to improve primary care services. I will also take the following courses:
 Creative Methods in Qualitative Research (2 days, Social Research Association, £220): course exploring
creativ
sectdars__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_c.text:
e methods of data collection and analysis.
 Forum Theatre Intensive Training (5 days, School of Theatre of the Oppressed, £850): intensive course to
develop skills in applied theatre methods.
Dissemination and impact
Patient and public involvement/engagement:
To support me in working with public contributors throughout the project I will take the following courses:
 Co-design, Co-production and Participatory Action Research; Inclusivity within Research Processes (1 day
each, University of Bristol)
 Hands on Engagement: Taking Research to Events and Festivals (1 day, QMUL): course to prepare me to
host the dissemination event.
Implementation of findings:
 Knowledge into Action (8 weeks part-time, University of Oxford, £3175), led by Professor Carl Heneghan.
This module will support my plans for impact and dissemination, helping me to understand how my
recommendations can be implemented in clinical practice. It will also inform an application for postdoctoral
funding to develop and evaluate interventions to improve blood test communication.
Disseminating findings:
 Science Communication Masterclass (4 days, UWE Bristol, £400) : intensive course on communicating
research findings
sectdars__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_d.text:
with the public. This will help me in sharing my PhD research via podcasts, blogs and
social media.
 WordPress website design (1 day, CityLit, £129): course to help me design a project website to share
study findings and patient resources.
Conferences:
I will attend and present work at national/international conferences including:
 Royal College of General Practitioners
 Society of Academic Primary Care
 World Organization of Family Doctors (WONCA)
Preventing Overdiagnosis: an international conference with a strong primary care presence

Development as a researcher
Leadership:
 Emerging Clinical Leaders (6 days in total, King’s Fund, £2712): interprofessional programme to build
skills in leadership, resilience and team management. This will further my development as an independent
research leader.
Teaching:
 To develop as an educator I will facilitate undergraduate/Masters module seminars within the department
when my research commitments allow. I will attend Graduate Teaching Assistant Development workshops
provided by QMUL.
Research networks:
I will be a member of the following research networks, allowing me to learn from other researchers, get feedback
on my work, and de
sectdars__001_d.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_e.text:
velop new collaborations:
At QMUL:
 APOLLO Social Sciences team:
o Multidisciplinary team of qualitative primary care researchers, led by Professor Swinglehurst.
Alongside regular team meetings I will have the opportunity to attend APOLLO’s termly writing
retreat.
 Thinking Between the Lines:
o Qualitative research network, co-convened by Dr Fudge, holding monthly meetings for
researchers to present work-in-progress and receive feedback.
 Curiosity Workshops:
Monthly gatherings to share and seek advice on qualitative research in-progress, led by Professor
o
Swinglehurst.
Externally:
 Preventing Overdiagnosis Early/Mid-Career Researcher Network:
o International network of researchers working on overdiagnosis, which provided helpful feedback
on this proposal.
 RCGP Optimal Testing group:
o Subgroup of the RCGP Overdiagnosis interest group, led by Dr Watson. I will attend their tri-yearly
meetings to share my work and discuss how to improve primary care blood testing practices.
Research visit
In the 3rd year of the PhD, I have been invited to spend six weeks in Australia visiting two research departments.
Both departments are pioneering work on the communication of overdiagnosis
sectdars__001_e.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_f.text:
to patients. In each site I will be
mentored by the unit lead and a mid-career researcher. We will agree a timetable in advance of my visit.
 Sydney Health Literacy Lab, University of Sydney
The Lab focuses on healthcare communication to prevent overdiagnosis and low-value care. I will
o
spend four weeks here, hosted by Professor Carissa Bonner, a behavioural scientist who
specialises in health literacy and risk communication, and Dr Brooke Nickel.
o I will use insights from the Lab’s extensive work co-producing health literacy tools to aid the
development of the WP4 resource for patients.
 Australian Centre for Health Engagement, Evidence and Values (ACHEEV), University of Wollongong.
o I will spend two weeks at ACHEEV, hosted by Professor Stacy Carter and Dr Patti Shih. The
ACHEEV team aims to find solutions to medical overuse by making evidence more accessible.
I am a technical advisor for Dr Shih’s project developing an online resource to explain blood test
o
results to patients, and will be able to develop this collaboration during my visit.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 1
Name Professor Deborah Swinglehurst
Institution Queen Mary, University of London
Position Professor of
sectdars__001_f.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_g.text:
Primary Care
Description of support provided
Deborah Swinglehurst is a Professor of Primary Care and general practitioner. Her academic work uses qualitative
methods, including ethnography and linguistic ethnography, to explore how interaction and social relationships
shape healthcare practices. She leads the APOLLO social science group, adopting innovative methodologies at
the interfaces of medicine, social science and linguistics to address complex healthcare challenges.
She is Institute co-lead for Applied Social Science and sits on the Executive Board and Research Committee. She
is in the senior management team for the Wellcome-funded Doctoral Training Programme on Health Data in
Practice and the leadership team for QMUL’s NIHR SPCR membership.
Professor Swinglehurst has published widely in journals including BMJ, BMJ Quality & Safety, BJGP, and Social
Science and Medicine. She is on the 2024 Stanford/Elsevier Top 2% Scientists list of the most cited scientists
globally and was awarded the 2019 RCGP/SAPC John Fry Award for her contribution to general practice. Her
recent NIHR-funded research on multimorbidity and polypharmacy involved co-producing a suite of RCGP e-
learning mo
sectdars__001_g.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_h.text:
dules (reaching >1000 learners) and a public engagement resource (Let’s Talk Differently about
Medicines) which has reached >1800 people.
She will provide expertise in meta-ethnography, ethnographic approaches (including linguistic ethnography/video-
reflexive ethnography), and creative co-production. We will have supervision meetings fortnightly and she will
provide further support as needed.
Accredited by the UK Council for Graduate Education as a recognised research supervisor, she has supervised 6
PhD students to completion, currently supervises 6 PhD students, and has examined 10 PhDs.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 2
Name Dr Jessica Watson
Institution University of Bristol
Position NIHR Academic Clinical Lecturer in General Practice
Description of support provided
Jessica Watson is a general practitioner and NIHR Academic Clinical Lecturer whose research uses mixed
methods to understand and improve primary care blood testing practices. She chairs the Primary Care Academic
Collaborative (PACT) and leads the Optimal Testing division of the RCGP Overdiagnosis group.
Dr Watson has been awarded three consecutive NIHR Fellowships and has delivered over £2m of research
funding. She h
sectdars__001_h.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_i.text:
as authored 64 peer-reviewed research articles which have been cited over 2,100 times. Her work
has been featured on BBC radio, ITV News, and in BMJ, BJGP and RCGP podcasts. She was Principal
Investigator on the NIHR-funded Test Talk Tools study (2022-2024), and currently leads two further NIHR-funded
studies:
 Accessible Results (Principal Investigator): qualitative project developing tools to help patients with diverse
needs to access and understand online blood test results.
 Optimal testing for long-term condition monitoring in primary care (Joint Lead Applicant): mixed-methods
study to develop, implement and evaluate a new blood testing strategy for long-term conditions.
Dr Watson will provide expertise in blood testing and overdiagnosis as well as mentorship in research
dissemination. She will support me to collaborate with researchers at the University of Bristol and within PACT. We
will meet online monthly, with twice-yearly meetings in-person in Bristol. We will have joint online meetings with
Professor Swinglehurst and Dr Fudge three times a year to ensure regular contact between all supervisors.
She is supervising 1 PhD student and has examined 3 PhDs. She has supervis
sectdars__001_i.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_j.text:
ed 2 GP ACFs and 3 MSc
students.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 3
Name Dr Nina Fudge
Institution Queen Mary, University of London
Position Lecturer in Social Science
Description of support provided
Dr Fudge is a Lecturer in Social Science and THIS Institute Research Fellow with a background in social
anthropology. Her research explores knowledge, expertise and practice in complex healthcare settings such as
community pharmacies and GP practices. Her recent research focuses on patients’ and clinicians’ experiences
and practices of polypharmacy, aiming to develop safe and person-centred approaches to primary care medicines
use.
She has extensive experience of qualitative participant-focused methods, including narrative, ethnographic and
visual approaches. She has particular expertise in video-reflexive ethnography (VRE) which she has used in
multiple polypharmacy research projects, including her THIS Fellowship (awarded 2021), which uses VRE to
improve the delivery of structured medication reviews.
She has worked closely with Professor Swinglehurst since 2017 as part of the APOLLO-MM project, including on
the co-production of patient-facing resources to encourage discussions around po
sectdars__001_j.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_k.text:
lypharmacy. She is Deputy
Director of Graduate Studies for the Wolfson Institute of Population Health and co-convenes Thinking Between the
Lines, a qualitative research network through which I will receive feedback on my work and take part in data
analysis workshops.
I will meet with Dr Fudge on a monthly basis throughout the fellowship. She will provide particular guidance on
hosting the VRE workshops in WP3 and analysing the resulting data. With her experience of co-design methods
she will also support me in co-producing the clinician and patient resources in WP4.
She is currently supervising 3 PhD students (1 with Professor Swinglehurst) and has supervised 4 PhD students to
completion (2 with Professor Swinglehurst).
Collaborations
Academic
Centre for Academic Primary Care (CAPC), University of Bristol
Through Dr Watson, I will develop links with CAPC and their ongoing blood testing research, including the
Accessible Results study – a participatory study exploring how people with diverse needs can be supported to
access blood test results online. Collaborating with CAPC will enable me to foster a cross-institutional partnership
on a project Professor Swinglehurst is developing w
sectdars__001_k.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_l.text:
hich explores GP blood testing organisational routines.
Primary care Academic Collaborative (PACT)
PACT – a network of UK primary care researchers – has run several studies relevant to my proposed PhD: the
Why Test study, investigating the rationale behind primary care blood tests; and the Hidden Workload study,
exploring clinician workload. I will collaborate with PACT to disseminate the research findings, including the
workshop for GP staff, and will explore the possibility of a national spin-off project exploring blood test
communication.
Sydney Health Literacy Lab/ Australian Centre for Health Engagement, Evidence and Values
These two centres – both part of Wiser Healthcare (https://www.wiserhealthcare.org.au/), a global initiative to
reduce overdiagnosis – will provide a rich environment for collaboration. My research visit will facilitate a mutual
exchange of methodological approaches. At the Sydney Health Literacy Lab, I will learn from behavioural
scientists about implementation science and patient resource development. At ACHEEV, I will explore their
empirical ethics work, particularly their use of community juries to evaluate overdiagnosis. In return, I will share my
expe
sectdars__001_l.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_m.text:
rience of using ethnographic methods to understand how overdiagnosis is discussed in clinical practice.
Clinical
Dr Stefán Hjörleifsson, Choosing Wisely Norway/University of Bergen
Dr Hjörleifsson, a Norwegian GP and academic, leads Choosing Wisely Norway, an initiative promoting
discussions between clinicians and patients about the necessity and value of investigations and treatments. There
is strong professional commitment to reducing unnecessary testing in Norway, and in 2016 the Norwegian College
of General Practice was the first national medical college worldwide to adopt a position on overdiagnosis. I am
collaborating with Dr Hjörleifsson on workshops for WONCA 2025 and the Nordic Congress of General Practice
2026. He has invited me to spend two weeks visiting him at the University of Bergen in the 2nd year of my PhD. I
will engage with their GP academic community, learn about their work advocating for prudent testing in Norway,
and explore how these strategies could be adapted to a UK context.
Lab Tests Online (UK), NHS Digital, and Pathology Tests Explained (Australia)
My supervisor Dr Watson works closely with Lab Tests Online, while my ACHEEV mentor, Dr Shih, is undertaki
sectdars__001_m.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_n.text:
ng
a project with Pathology Tests Explained (the Australian counterpart), on which I am a technical advisor. These
organisations provide online resources to help patients understand their blood test results. Dr Watson is also
collaborating with NHS Digital on the Accessible Results study, to provide guidance on NHS app design for online
results access. I will work with these three organisations to integrate my research findings and offer
recommendations of how online blood test resources could be improved.
Community
Social Action for Health (SAfH): East London community health charity.
SAfH have extensive experience running community health research projects and focus groups with NHS and
academic partners. I will work with SAfH to assemble participants for the Forum Theatre workshops in WP4.
Improbable: Improvisation-based theatre company (https://www.improbable.co.uk/)
Known for its award-winning productions, Improbable also operates a research wing that uses improvisation to
interpret research findings and bring them to new audiences. Ellie Claughton, Improbable’s Executive Director, has
agreed to collaborate on the project. Improbable’s theatre-makers will help to design the pla
sectdars__001_n.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_o.text:
y and facilitate the
Forum Theatre workshops.
Host Organisation Support Statement
The following statement of support must be completed by the head of your lead academic department. In order to
contribute to this application, this individual must be invited to do so via the 'Participants and Signatories' section of
the form.
Statement of support
As Deputy Director of Wolfson Institute of Population Health (WIPH), I am delighted to support Lotte’s application
for an NIHR Doctoral Fellowship.
Lotte has already made significant contributions to academic primary care with her ethnographic study of stigma
experienced by homeless people during her GP Academic Clinical Fellowship at King’s College London. She has
won numerous prizes, has a strong publication record, and has already accrued considerable expertise in
leadership, teaching and management. She clearly has aptitude for ethnographic methodologies, interdisciplinary
scholarship, and a passion for primary care. A Fellowship will enable her to grow as an independent researcher.
Her proposal extends research being conducted in both our Centre for Primary Care and our Centre for Cancer
Screening, Prevention and Early Diagnosis and ali
sectdars__001_o.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_p.text:
gns with our commitment to our cross-cutting theme (co-led by
Professor Deborah Swinglehurst) on Applied Social Science in Health. Lotte has developed her application over
many months, meeting regularly with Prof. Swinglehurst and Dr Nina Fudge, and has twice joined their group on
writing retreats to support this development.
The Centre for Primary Care (CPC), where Lotte will be based, is a vibrant, multidisciplinary group with interests
spanning multimorbidity, complex interventions, data science, health inequalities, sustainable environments,
healthcare interactions, and complex systems. The CPC specialises in adopting methodologies - quantitative and
qualitative - that address the complexities of generalist clinical practice.
The CPC leads the university’s membership of the prestigious NIHR School for Primary Care Research, which
focuses on facilitating high quality research through strategic leadership and ringfenced funding, and on building
capacity through an established training programme. Lotte will have access to the training resources, networks
and opportunities across the nine HEI members of the SPCR, with strong links to the NIHR Schools for Social
Care Research and Pu
sectdars__001_p.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_q.text:
blic Health Research. We will also support Lotte in developing a post-doctoral programme
of work. Prof. Swinglehurst has supported recently completed PhD students with successful post-doctoral
Fellowships, seed/bridging awards, mentorship, honorary academic titles and grant-writing retreats.
SPCR membership offers numerous opportunities for collaboration across HEIs. This proposal marks an exciting
new collaboration with Lotte’s secondary supervisor, Dr Jessica Watson (Bristol), an academic GP who has
published widely on investigations in primary care. It has already sparked the possibility of further QMUL-Bristol
collaborations to research overdiagnosis and medical overuse – an issue of pressing concern in the quest for
sustainable primary care.
Lotte’s training will be overseen by an experienced supervisory team. Prof. Swinglehurst has an international
reputation for qualitative research in primary care, with special expertise in ethnography and narrative
methodologies. She has a strong record of capacity building through supervision of academic Fellows (at pre-
doctoral/doctoral/post-doctoral level), primary and secondary care clinicians, allied health professionals, social
scie
sectdars__001_q.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_r.text:
ntists, and anthropologists. She leads the APOLLO group, with 2-hour weekly meetings in which members
collaborate to provide peer support, encouragement and constructive feedback on work-in-progress.
