WIPH currently hosts 130 postgraduate research students. This includes two Wellcome-funded Doctoral Training
Programmes (Health Data in Practice (HDIP)); Primary Care Clinicians). Prof. Swinglehurst is co-director of the
HDIP programme and Dr Fudge is an Associate Director. The Institute also recruits doctoral students via the Trials
Methodology Research Partnership DTP, the School for Primary Care Research PhD studentship scheme, the
London Interdisciplinary Social Sciences DTP, the City of London Cancer Centre PhD studentships, and other
national and international schemes. Lotte will therefore be well-supported by a network of peers and mentors.
Academic support for WIPH PGRs is led by our excellent Graduate Studies team (Dr Fudge is Deputy Director)
and our Graduate School offers induction and ongoing support including personal development workshops,
wellbeing and counselling, travel awards, training events.
WIPH provides regular opportunities for intellectual discussion, including
sectdars__001_r.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_s.text:
the Early Careers Research Group,
whiteboard sessions, seminars, the ‘Curiosity Workshop’ (qualitative researchers discussing work-in-progress) and
the ‘Thinking Between the Lines’ critical theory group. Students can also present work at the annual WIPH
graduate studies event and the Faculty William Harvey Day.
The QMUL Centre for Academic and Professional Development provides a comprehensive programme of short
courses and bespoke support. These include writing and presentation skills, personal organisation, academic
skills, career planning, and media training. The Centre for Public Engagement also provides funding to support
public engagement and hosts the annual ‘Festival of Communities’ event in east London.
QMUL offers an unparalleled environment for this Fellowship. QMUL’s research outputs, impact performance and
research environment are all highly competitive as assessed in the 2021 Research Excellence Framework. We
were ranked 7th in the UK for quality. Within the Faculty of Medicine and Dentistry (FMD) in Clinical Medicine 87%
of outputs were ranked 3*-4*, 97% of impact statements were ranked 3*-4*, and 100% of the environment was
ranked 3*- 4*. In Public Health, Health Ser
sectdars__001_s.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_t.text:
vices and Primary Care, 89% of outputs were ranked 3*-4*, with 100%
of outputs ranked 3*-4* in both environment and impact case studies.
QMUL is one of the most ethnically diverse research-led universities in the world. Commitment to equality,
diversity and inclusion (EDI) is embedded into all day-to-day and strategic decision-making within the Faculty,
reflected in our research aims (‘Every life is precious’). This work is overseen by six staff leads, who coordinate a
network of academic and professional services staff with recognised roles within each institute. We proactively
embed EDI into all meeting structures, policies and procedures. Faculty and institute-level leadership committees
include EDI representation, and institute EDI reps ensure that faculty-level initiatives result in meaningful outcomes
as well as developing and leading local change. FMD was the first QMUL faculty to receive a SWAN Bronze award
(2008) and recently achieved Gold (2024).
FMD has a Flexible Research Fund scheme through which 20% of overheads earned on any FEC grant/award is
returned to the investigator. Lotte would be able to use this for approved research-related activity (e.g. training,
conferen
sectdars__001_t.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_u.text:
ce attendance, organising a symposium).
Developing our PhD students is pivotal to our contribution to research and education locally, nationally and
internationally. QMUL would be delighted to host Lotte with this prestigious Award. We wish her every success.
sectdars__001_u.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

secsb__001_a.text:
The finance section should provide a breakdown of costs associated with undertaking the research as
described in the proposal.
1
Staff type Lead Applicant
Name Lotte Elton
Employing
Queen Mary University of London
organisation
Grade Clinical Academic (C12)
Starting point on scale 20
Increment Date 01/10/2026
Justification of Costs
Staff costs
At the time of taking up the award I will be a fully-qualified GP (CCT date August 2025). In discussion with the
finance team I have been placed on the QMUL paygrade (C12, spinal point 20) which most closely matches point
1 on the BMA pre-2003 consultant payscale (https://www.bma.org.uk/pay-and-contracts/pay/consultants-pay-
scales/pay-scales-for-consultants-in-england). The salary requested includes London weighting (as I will be based
in London throughout the fellowship) and the QMUL apprenticeship levy (included with London weighting in "other
allowances").
Travel and subsistence
The research will be based in London, where I live, so direct travel costs are minimal. I have included funding to
travel to Bristol twice a year to meet my supervisor, Dr Watson, in person. Many of the courses included in the
training plan are in London, so no tra

secsb__001_b.text:
vel costs are required for these. Where training courses are outside London
(e.g. Bristol, Oxford, Essex) I have included funding for standard-class rail fares, taking into account that I may
have to travel at peak times (e.g. early in the morning to save on accommodation costs the previous night). Where
a course outside of London lasts for more than one day, I have included funding for accommodation in standard
business hotels at a rate of £100/night. This is within the limit for accommodation set by QMUL. Where there are
cheaper options for accommodation (e.g. at the University of Essex), I have adjusted accommodation costs
accordingly. I have included funding for meals at £30/day where I will be staying overnight in hotels.
I have requested funding for a 6-week research visit to Australia as well as a 2-week visit to Norway to allow me to
develop my collaboration with Dr Stefan Hjörleifsson. All costed travel is standard class at approximate current
rates. In both Australia and Norway I would plan to stay in standard business accommodation with cooking
facilities (e.g. an aparthotel) as this means I would not require further subsistence costs. As I would be staying for
a longer

secsb__001_c.text:
duration, accommodation costs would be <£100/night.
Equipment
I have requested funding for a digital voice recorder (for recording ethnographic interviews) and a video camera
(for recording consultations). If I am able to I will borrow these from QMUL, but the length of time I will be spending
in the field means that it may not be practical to use shared university equipment. I will need an encrypted hard
drive to store this data and have included funding for a second back-up hard drive to prevent loss of data if the
principal hard drive breaks or is lost. On the advice of my supervisors, who have done video-reflexive ethnography
workshops before, I have included funding for a video projector. This can be used when showing video clips during
video-reflexive ethnography workshops and avoids any potential difficulties using encrypted hard drives on NHS
computers.
Consumables
These costs include funding for printing consent forms and information leaflets/flyers. These will be distributed in
research sites to introduce the research as well as in local GP practices to share the research findings.
PPI
I have embedded robust Patient and Public Involvement throughout the proposal. Public c

secsb__001_d.text:
ontributors will play a
significant role not only in steering the research plan but also in contributing to the findings and dissemination of
the research. I have budgeted for three meetings per year with the PPI panel, including travel expenses,
refreshments, and childcare/care expenses as needed. I have budgeted separately for PPI contributors to
participate in the day-long WP4 workshops to design and disseminate research outputs.
Two members of the public with extensive PPI experience will co-chair the Project Advisory Group. Both co-chairs
were keen to attend some of the planned events (e.g. the Forum Theatre workshops) and wanted to meet in-
person if possible. I have therefore budgeted for one in-person meeting per year.
I have included a training fund for PPI contributors (including the PPI panel and the lay co-chairs) to access
training relevant to the research. I anticipate that the lay co-chairs may attend management training (e.g. on
chairing meetings), and that some of the PPI panel may train in Forum Theatre methods alongside me. The choice
of training courses will be made in discussion with PPI contributors.
In order to recruit local participants for the Forum Theatre

secsb__001_e.text:
workshops, I have included funding for Social Action for
Health, a community health charity in East London which has worked closely with QMUL researchers for several
years. They usually charge approximately £2,000 per focus group (with translators) so the £5,000 fee should cover
two Forum Theatre workshops. They have longstanding links within the local community, including with people not
usually reached by research (e.g. those who do not speak English as a first language). I will liaise with Social
Action for Health to ensure that the Forum Theatre workshops are as representative of the local community as
possible.
Dissemination costs
The fees paid to actors and to Improbable (who will help in recruiting actors and can also help to publicise the
resources) will ensure that the Forum Theatre workshops are conducted by trained professionals with experience
in improvisation and participatory theatre. I have been advised by Improbable to cost in £4,000 to compensate
them for their staff members’ time. I have set actors’ fees at £300/day, roughly in line with Equity (the actors’
union) industry standards for independent productions.
I have budgeted £6,000 in total to allow for the dev

secsb__001_f.text:
elopment of the resources for patients and clinicians (with
separate fees for actors/public contributors). The £6,000 will cover fees for filming and licensing the short video
clips, post-production costs, and website editing/design fees. I have budgeted a reasonable amount for these
costs to ensure that the resources look professional and are user-friendly.

implications: Applicant CV: Lists formal qualifications (MPhil, MSc, MBBS) and research roles (Honorary Research Fellow), demonstrating relevant training and experience.
Applicant Research Background: Details extensive experience in qualitative methods (ethnography, interviews) and formal training (MPhil, Diploma, PGCert), aligning with project needs.
Applicant CV: Lists 7 publications in peer-reviewed journals and multiple awards (e.g., Sydney Selwyn Prize, Oral Presentation Prize), indicating strong research outputs.
Applicant Research Background: Lists multiple high-impact publications (e.g., top 5% Altmetric), awards (Sydney Selwyn prize), and grants, demonstrating strong research outputs.
Applicant CV: Shows increasing responsibility through leadership roles (Policy Director, Co-Chair) and recognition via teaching and leadership awards.
Applicant Research Background: Shows increasing responsibility through leading research strands, organizing conferences, and holding advisory board roles.
Applicant CV: Demonstrates a practitioner-academic career with clinical roles (GP Registrar, Locum Doctor) alongside research positions and clinical research awards.
Applicant Research Background: Explicitly identifies as a GP trainee and Academic GP trainee, demonstrating a clear commitment to a practitioner-academic career.
Applicant Research Background: Clearly articulates a research vision focused on medical overuse and a career trajectory aiming for a Professor of Primary Care role.
Applicant Research Background: States the fellowship will develop complex qualitative methodologies and strengthen the portfolio, explaining why this award is the right next step.
Applicant Research Background: Provides detailed evidence of prior successful projects in overdiagnosis, stigma, and diabetes prevention, relevant to the proposed work.
Applicant Research Background: Demonstrates experience working with underserved groups including homeless people and LGBT communities in prior research projects.
Detailed Research Plan: Plans to recruit from contrasting practices (inner-city multi-ethnic vs. suburban older) and include patients with multimorbidity and mental illness.

Patient & Public Involvement: Highlights involvement of Black British, South Asian, Chinese, disabled, and neurodivergent participants, and partnership with a charity serving East London communities, supporting wp.4.
Plain English Summary of Research: The section provides a clear problem statement, objectives, methods, and beneficiaries in accessible language, supporting pr.1.

Scientific Abstract: Clearly outlines the problem (blood test overuse), aims, methods (ethnography, workshops), and impact (co-produced resources), fulfilling the requirements for a comprehensive plain English summary.
Plain English NLP Analysis: NLP metrics show high readability (Flesch-Kincaid 6.76, 0% long sentences) and low jargon density (3.99%), strongly supporting the quality of the Plain English Summary.
Plain English Summary of Research: The text identifies knowledge gaps regarding patient understanding and communication limitations, supporting pr.2.
Scientific Abstract: Identifies a specific knowledge gap where NHS guidelines focus on test appropriateness but neglect communication strategies, justifying the need for this research.

Detailed Research Plan: Critically reviews existing literature on blood test overuse and communication gaps, identifying a specific lack of naturalistic data on GP-patient interactions to justify the study.
Plain English Summary of Research: The four-part research design with specific tasks (evidence search, observation, workshops) indicates feasibility and appropriate scope, supporting pr.3.
Scientific Abstract: Defines a feasible scope with four distinct work packages (meta-ethnography, observation, video-reflexive workshops, Forum Theatre) appropriate for a fellowship project.
Detailed Research Plan: Outlines a feasible four-work package structure with specific timelines (e.g., 4-6 months observation) and clear objectives, demonstrating appropriate scope for a fellowship.
Plain English Summary of Research: Explicit methods including evidence search, observation, and theatre workshops are described, aligning with aims to improve communication, supporting pr.4.
Scientific Abstract: Explicitly details a multi-method design including meta-ethnography, ethnographic observation, video-reflexive ethnography, and Forum Theatre, demonstrating methodological rigor.

Detailed Research Plan: Provides explicit methodological descriptions for meta-ethnography, ethnography, video-reflexive ethnography, and Forum Theatre, with rigorous data collection and analysis plans.
Plain English Summary of Research: The project aims to improve patient safety, involvement, and communication, with outputs like an online resource and staff workshop, supporting pr.5.
Scientific Abstract: Specifies tangible outputs including a co-produced workshop for clinicians and an online resource for patients to improve blood test communication and reduce overuse.
Detailed Research Plan: Details specific outputs including clinician workshops and patient online resources, with clear anticipated impacts on patient safety, cost savings, and reduced overuse.
Plain English Summary of Research: Public contributors are involved in shaping the project, creating a play, and guiding research via a PPI panel, supporting pr.6.
Scientific Abstract: Describes active engagement through participatory methods, including Forum Theatre workshops with members of the public and co-production of resources.
Detailed Research Plan: Describes deep engagement via PPI workshops, Forum Theatre with public participants, and co-production of resources, ensuring community involvement throughout the project.
Plain English Summary of Research: The PPI panel includes contributors with diverse ages, ethnic backgrounds, and health experiences, supporting pr.9.
Scientific Abstract: Mentions ethnographic observation in practices with 'contrasting patient populations,' indicating an intention to capture diverse perspectives.
Detailed Research Plan: Explicitly plans for diverse recruitment across age, ethnicity, and health status, including interpreters and translation of consent forms to ensure inclusivity.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Allocates funds for a community charity to recruit non-English speakers and for translating materials into 6 languages, supporting inclusive recruitment strategies.
Plain English Summary of Research: Lived experience is integrated through public contributors shaping the project and co-designing a play for communication tools, supporting wp.1.
Scientific Abstract: Integrates lived experience by engaging members of the public in Forum Theatre workshops to co-produce strategies for improving communication.
Detailed Research Plan: Integrates lived experience by using PPI feedback to shape site selection and co-designing Forum Theatre scenarios with public participants.

Patient & Public Involvement: Describes specific design changes based on workshop feedback, such as excluding GPs from play development sessions and integrating PPG observations, directly supporting wp.1.
Plain English Summary of Research: Dissemination plans include social media, blogs, podcasts, public events, and training tools for staff, supporting wp.3.
Scientific Abstract: Outlines a robust dissemination plan including academic journals, conferences, blogs, podcasts, and publicly accessible resources for patients and staff.
Detailed Research Plan: Outlines a comprehensive dissemination strategy including academic papers, conferences, podcasts, and public-facing resources to maximize benefit.

Distinctive ideas with an independent direction

sub_criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear research vision focused on medical overuse and communication, with a distinct career trajectory towards a Professor of Primary Care role (g.1.a, g.1.b). The training plan is exceptionally well-justified, mapping specific courses (e.g., Forum Theatre, Advanced Qualitative Research) directly to project work packages and skill gaps (g.1.c). evidence.text:
aisal module for Global Health BSc students at QMUL. I was
also awarded the Foundation School Award for Teaching Excellence in recognition of my mentorship of medical
students applying for Academic Foundation posts. I have held Associate Fellowship of the Higher Education
Academy since 2019.
From 2015-2016, I was Policy and Advocacy Director for Students for Global Health UK, leading a team of policy
analysts and steering four national student research collaborations. As chair of the Cambridge Global Health
Lecture Committee (2019-2020) I coordinated a series of six public lectures with over 200 attendees.
Impact of fellowship
A Doctoral Research Fellowship will develop my skills in complex qualitative methodologies and strengthen my
portfolio of research addressing medical overuse. Following a PhD, I aim to secure an Academic Clinical
Lectureship to conduct further work exploring the social and relational factors influencing decision-making in
primary care, focusing on how to navigate uncertainty and foster shared understanding between patients and
clinicians. I am especially interested in co-producing creative outputs to engage wider audiences in considering
how medical resources

can be used wisely. My eventual goal is to become a Professor of Primary Care, using
interdisciplinary qualitative methods to inform excellence in primary care delivery.
Have you already
No
registered for a PhD?
Has this application, or a similar application, previously been submitted to this or any other funding body?
Yes
If Yes, provide details
I have submitted an application to the Wellcome-funded NIHR School of Primary Care Research Primary Care
Clinicians PhD Programme. I do not yet know whether I have been invited to interview.
Contextual Factors
Please use this section to detail any factors that may have impacted the development of your research career or
outputs from your research career to date. NIHR wants to know about any circumstances so that they may take
them into consideration during the assessment of your application. Contextual factors may include:
● Career breaks due to parental leave, or periods of illness.
● Reduced time spent undertaking research activities (including research outputs), due to disability or to
caring responsibilities. This may include physical or mental health related impairments or conditions, which have
impacted your opportunities to underta

Proposed training and development programme
The QMUL Doctoral College and the Wolfson Institute of Population Health offer doctoral development
programmes with courses covering project management, personal effectiveness, and research governance. I will
attend induction events, yearly cohort days, and research skills seminars. As outlined below and in the attached
training timetable, I have planned further training to allow me to successfully complete the PhD and continue my
development as a clinical researcher.
Research skills
Qualitative methodology:
 Advanced Qualitative Research Methods (8 weeks part-time, University of Oxford, £3175): Masters
module focusing on more complex aspects of qualitative research such as reflexivity, bias, and advanced
data collection methods. This will underpin the qualitative methods training I received in my MSc and
MPhil.
Meta-ethnography (WP1):
To refresh my skills in systematic review and develop my understanding of qualitative synthesis, I will take the
following courses:
 Literature Searching for Systematic Reviews (half-day, QMUL): course on literature searching in
preparation for the meta-ethnography database searches.
 Overview of Qualita
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
tive Review and Synthesis Methods (1 day, University of Bristol): course to aid me in
conducting an interpretive qualitative synthesis.
Ethnography and video-reflexive ethnography (WPs2&3):
Building on my previous experience of ethnographic research, I will take the following external courses:
 Ethnography and Ethnographic Methods (5 days, Essex Summer School in Social Science Data Analysis,
£1,600): module to provide a thorough introduction to the theory and practice of ethnographic research
and set me up for ethnographic observation in GP practices.
 Summer school module on Linguistic Ethnography (5 days, Ghent University, £250): an introduction to key
concepts in linguistic ethnography. This will support me in the close analysis of recorded data in WP2,
building on the Conversation Analysis course (University of Oxford) I completed in 2024.
Creative co-production (WP4):
I will be mentored by Dr Jessica Drinkwater, a member of the Project Advisory Group who has experience of using
forum theatre to improve primary care services. I will also take the following courses:
 Creative Methods in Qualitative Research (2 days, Social Research Association, £220): course exploring
creativ
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
e methods of data collection and analysis.
 Forum Theatre Intensive Training (5 days, School of Theatre of the Oppressed, £850): intensive course to
develop skills in applied theatre methods.
Dissemination and impact
Patient and public involvement/engagement:
To support me in working with public contributors throughout the project I will take the following courses:
 Co-design, Co-production and Participatory Action Research; Inclusivity within Research Processes (1 day
each, University of Bristol)
 Hands on Engagement: Taking Research to Events and Festivals (1 day, QMUL): course to prepare me to
host the dissemination event.
Implementation of findings:
 Knowledge into Action (8 weeks part-time, University of Oxford, £3175), led by Professor Carl Heneghan.
This module will support my plans for impact and dissemination, helping me to understand how my
recommendations can be implemented in clinical practice. It will also inform an application for postdoctoral
funding to develop and evaluate interventions to improve blood test communication.
Disseminating findings:
 Science Communication Masterclass (4 days, UWE Bristol, £400) : intensive course on communicating
research findings

sub_criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
sub_criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
sub_criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant clearly articulates why this fellowship is the right next step, stating it will develop complex qualitative methodologies and strengthen their portfolio to secure postdoctoral funding (g.2.a, g.2.b). The award builds on existing experience in overdiagnosis and stigma research while enabling new skills in linguistic ethnography and creative methods (g.2.c). It explicitly develops partnerships with the NHS, charities (Social Action for Health), and theatre groups (Improbable) (g.2.d).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the justification for the award's necessity is comprehensive and well-linked to career progression.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the justification for the award's necessity is comprehensive and well-linked to career progression.

Please describe your research career to date
I am a qualitative primary care researcher with interests in medical overuse, risk communication, and the role of
evidence and values in medical care. I have an academic background (MSc/MPhil) spanning medical sociology,
philosophy, and public health. My research is particularly concerned with the social meanings that patients and
clinicians ascribe to diagnosis. I have published my research in leading journals, including Sociology of Health and
Illness and Journal of Medical Ethics, and have presented widely nationally and internationally. I have held NIHR
academic fellowships throughout clinical training: first as an Academic Foundation doctor and then as an academic
GP trainee.
Overdiagnosis research (2019-present)
My MPhil (see below) sparked my interest in how clinicians negotiate risk and uncertainty and how they
communicate these issues to patients. I published a single-author paper in Journal of Medical Ethics on how to
explain the pitfalls of s

creening to patients (top 5% of all Altmetric-scored outputs). I was invited to present this
work at Preventing Overdiagnosis 2022.
I then completed a Diploma in Philosophy of Medicine, exploring in my dissertation how clinicians balance risk
when diagnosing patients as dying. This was awarded the Sydney Selwyn prize for the best Diploma dissertation. I
was invited to give a public lecture on my research and published it as a single-author paper in Journal of
Evaluation in Clinical Practice (2022). I presented this work at Preventing Overdiagnosis 2023.
I belong to the Preventing Overdiagnosis Early Career Researchers group and am part of the pre-conference
organising committee for the 2025 conference.

creening to patients (top 5% of all Altmetric-scored outputs). I was invited to present this
work at Preventing Overdiagnosis 2022.
I then completed a Diploma in Philosophy of Medicine, exploring in my dissertation how clinicians balance risk
when diagnosing patients as dying. This was awarded the Sydney Selwyn prize for the best Diploma dissertation. I
was invited to give a public lecture on my research and published it as a single-author paper in Journal of
Evaluation in Clinical Practice (2022). I presented this work at Preventing Overdiagnosis 2023.
I belong to the Preventing Overdiagnosis Early Career Researchers group and am part of the pre-conference
organising committee for the 2025 conference.
NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship, King's College London (2021-Aug 2025)
I have been a Research Fellow on Dr Andy Guise’s UKRI-funded Social Responses to Stigma project since
beginning my academic placement in 2023. This ethnographic study examines how stigma and discrimination
arise within the health and care services used by people who are homeless in south London. My work includes
ethnographic observation in day centres and collaborative analysis of fieldnotes and interviews. I le

ad a research
strand exploring intersections between stigma, homelessness, and health self-management.
Outputs include:
 Presentation at the RCGP Conference 2024.
 First-author paper in preparation (for submission to Sociology of Health and Illness).
 Co-author on a quantitative survey of homeless people’s stigma experiences, (under review, BMJ Open).
 Co-author of a forthcoming submission on drug use stigma (International Journal of Drug Policy).
In 2022, I collaborated with researchers at Charité University Berlin and Medical University of Vienna on a project
to improve Type 2 diabetes prevention in women with gestational diabetes. We secured a £10,000 European
University Hospitals Alliance grant and convened workshops in London, Berlin and Vienna. I am first author of our
interdisciplinary commentary paper (provisionally accepted January 2025, Journal of Midwifery and Women’s
Health).
Other activities:
 PGCert in Applied Research Methods (KCL) (completion June 2025). Awarded Distinction in all completed
modules.
 Advisory board member (2022-2024) for PREPARED, an interventional primary care study to improve
pregnancy outcomes in women with diabetes.

Please describe your research career to date
I am a qualitative primary care researcher with interests in medical overuse, risk communication, and the role of
evidence and values in medical care. I have an academic background (MSc/MPhil) spanning medical sociology,
philosophy, and public health. My research is particularly concerned with the social meanings that patients and
clinicians ascribe to diagnosis. I have published my research in leading journals, including Sociology of Health and
Illness and Journal of Medical Ethics, and have presented widely nationally and internationally. I have held NIHR
academic fellowships throughout clinical training: first as an Academic Foundation doctor and then as an academic
GP trainee.
Overdiagnosis research (2019-present)
My MPhil (see below) sparked my interest in how clinicians negotiate risk and uncertainty and how they
communicate these issues to patients. I published a single-author paper in Journal of Medical Ethics on how to
explain the pitfalls of s

creening to patients (top 5% of all Altmetric-scored outputs). I was invited to present this
work at Preventing Overdiagnosis 2022.
I then completed a Diploma in Philosophy of Medicine, exploring in my dissertation how clinicians balance risk
when diagnosing patients as dying. This was awarded the Sydney Selwyn prize for the best Diploma dissertation. I
was invited to give a public lecture on my research and published it as a single-author paper in Journal of
Evaluation in Clinical Practice (2022). I presented this work at Preventing Overdiagnosis 2023.
I belong to the Preventing Overdiagnosis Early Career Researchers group and am part of the pre-conference
organising committee for the 2025 conference.

arise within the health and care services used by people who are homeless in south London. My work includes

criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear research vision focused on medical overuse and communication, with a distinct career trajectory towards a Professor of Primary Care role (g.1.a, g.1.b). The training plan is exceptionally well-justified, mapping specific courses (e.g., Forum Theatre, Advanced Qualitative Research) directly to project work packages and skill gaps (g.1.c). Strong mentorship is evidenced by a detailed supervisory framework including fortnightly meetings and a Project Advisory Group of experts, supported by institutional resources at QMUL (g.1.d).
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks identified; the evidence for all signals is robust and explicitly grounded in the application text.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks identified; the evidence for all signals is robust and explicitly grounded in the application text.

evidence.text:
aisal module for Global Health BSc students at QMUL. I was
also awarded the Foundation School Award for Teaching Excellence in recognition of my mentorship of medical
students applying for Academic Foundation posts. I have held Associate Fellowship of the Higher Education
Academy since 2019.
From 2015-2016, I was Policy and Advocacy Director for Students for Global Health UK, leading a team of policy
analysts and steering four national student research collaborations. As chair of the Cambridge Global Health
Lecture Committee (2019-2020) I coordinated a series of six public lectures with over 200 attendees.
Impact of fellowship
A Doctoral Research Fellowship will develop my skills in complex qualitative methodologies and strengthen my
portfolio of research addressing medical overuse. Following a PhD, I aim to secure an Academic Clinical
Lectureship to conduct further work exploring the social and relational factors influencing decision-making in
primary care, focusing on how to navigate uncertainty and foster shared understanding between patients and
clinicians. I am especially interested in co-producing creative outputs to engage wider audiences in considering
how medical resources

can be used wisely. My eventual goal is to become a Professor of Primary Care, using
interdisciplinary qualitative methods to inform excellence in primary care delivery.
Have you already
No
registered for a PhD?
Has this application, or a similar application, previously been submitted to this or any other funding body?
Yes
If Yes, provide details
I have submitted an application to the Wellcome-funded NIHR School of Primary Care Research Primary Care
Clinicians PhD Programme. I do not yet know whether I have been invited to interview.
Contextual Factors
Please use this section to detail any factors that may have impacted the development of your research career or
outputs from your research career to date. NIHR wants to know about any circumstances so that they may take
them into consideration during the assessment of your application. Contextual factors may include:
● Career breaks due to parental leave, or periods of illness.
● Reduced time spent undertaking research activities (including research outputs), due to disability or to
caring responsibilities. This may include physical or mental health related impairments or conditions, which have
impacted your opportunities to underta

Proposed training and development programme
The QMUL Doctoral College and the Wolfson Institute of Population Health offer doctoral development
programmes with courses covering project management, personal effectiveness, and research governance. I will
attend induction events, yearly cohort days, and research skills seminars. As outlined below and in the attached
training timetable, I have planned further training to allow me to successfully complete the PhD and continue my
development as a clinical researcher.
Research skills
Qualitative methodology:
 Advanced Qualitative Research Methods (8 weeks part-time, University of Oxford, £3175): Masters
module focusing on more complex aspects of qualitative research such as reflexivity, bias, and advanced
data collection methods. This will underpin the qualitative methods training I received in my MSc and
MPhil.
Meta-ethnography (WP1):
To refresh my skills in systematic review and develop my understanding of qualitative synthesis, I will take the
following courses:
 Literature Searching for Systematic Reviews (half-day, QMUL): course on literature searching in
preparation for the meta-ethnography database searches.
 Overview of Qualita
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
tive Review and Synthesis Methods (1 day, University of Bristol): course to aid me in
conducting an interpretive qualitative synthesis.
Ethnography and video-reflexive ethnography (WPs2&3):
Building on my previous experience of ethnographic research, I will take the following external courses:
 Ethnography and Ethnographic Methods (5 days, Essex Summer School in Social Science Data Analysis,
£1,600): module to provide a thorough introduction to the theory and practice of ethnographic research
and set me up for ethnographic observation in GP practices.
 Summer school module on Linguistic Ethnography (5 days, Ghent University, £250): an introduction to key
concepts in linguistic ethnography. This will support me in the close analysis of recorded data in WP2,
building on the Conversation Analysis course (University of Oxford) I completed in 2024.
Creative co-production (WP4):
I will be mentored by Dr Jessica Drinkwater, a member of the Project Advisory Group who has experience of using
forum theatre to improve primary care services. I will also take the following courses:
 Creative Methods in Qualitative Research (2 days, Social Research Association, £220): course exploring
creativ
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
e methods of data collection and analysis.
 Forum Theatre Intensive Training (5 days, School of Theatre of the Oppressed, £850): intensive course to
develop skills in applied theatre methods.
Dissemination and impact
Patient and public involvement/engagement:
To support me in working with public contributors throughout the project I will take the following courses:
 Co-design, Co-production and Participatory Action Research; Inclusivity within Research Processes (1 day
each, University of Bristol)
 Hands on Engagement: Taking Research to Events and Festivals (1 day, QMUL): course to prepare me to
host the dissemination event.
Implementation of findings:
 Knowledge into Action (8 weeks part-time, University of Oxford, £3175), led by Professor Carl Heneghan.
This module will support my plans for impact and dissemination, helping me to understand how my
recommendations can be implemented in clinical practice. It will also inform an application for postdoctoral
funding to develop and evaluate interventions to improve blood test communication.
Disseminating findings:
 Science Communication Masterclass (4 days, UWE Bristol, £400) : intensive course on communicating
research findings

criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

criteria.pros: The applicant clearly articulates why this fellowship is the right next step, stating it will develop complex qualitative methodologies and strengthen their portfolio to secure postdoctoral funding (g.2.a, g.2.b). The award builds on existing experience in overdiagnosis and stigma research while enabling new skills in linguistic ethnography and creative methods (g.2.c). It explicitly develops partnerships with the NHS, charities (Social Action for Health), and theatre groups (Improbable) (g.2.d).
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the justification for the award's necessity is comprehensive and well-linked to career progression.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the justification for the award's necessity is comprehensive and well-linked to career progression.

evidence.text:
Background
Communicating effectively about blood testing is important for ensuring patient safety and involvement in
healthcare. Blood tests are useful, but do not always improve patient care. Sometimes they can cause harm: for
example, borderline test results may worry patients or lead to unnecessary further tests. This is important because
blood tests have become increasingly common: general practice (GP) testing rates have tripled since 2000.
Previous research has shown that patients often feel unsure about why blood tests are done and what results
mean. GP staff do not always clearly explain the reasons for testing and the limitations of blood tests, and patients
report not having enough opportunity to speak to staff about testing. This means that GP staff and patients may
have different expectations and understandings of blood tests. Understanding what happens in consultations
about blood tests is important for addressing the rise in testing and ensuring tests are used wisely.
Aims
This project will explore how blood tests are discussed between patients and GP staff, aiming to improve
communication about the limitations and risks of blood tests. The goal is for patients to be
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
more involved in
decisions about blood testing and understand better what blood tests mean for their health.
Research design
The project has four parts:
1. Evidence search: I will search and review existing research on how blood tests are discussed in general
practices. This will help to understand what is already known and what is missing.
2. Observation at GP practices: I will spend several months watching conversations about blood tests at two
different general practices. I will make recordings of patient consultations with GP staff (including GPs,
nurses, pharmacists and phlebotomists) to look at how blood tests are discussed.
3. Workshops with GP staff: I will show short recordings of blood test discussions to staff. They will consider
how blood tests are discussed with patients and think about how communication can be improved.
4. Theatre workshops with the public: Professional actors and public contributors will create a short play
based on the research. This will be performed to members of the public as part of “Forum Theatre”
workshops. Workshop attendees will be invited to make changes to the play to improve communication
about blood tests. Public contributors will help t
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
o develop the play into two tools to improve blood test
communication:
a. A workshop for general practice staff.
b. An online education resource for patients.
Patient and Public Involvement (PPI)
People who have recently had blood tests will be involved throughout the project. Nineteen public contributors with
diverse ages, ethnic backgrounds, and health experiences helped to shape the project over three workshops.
They described many issues with communication about blood tests and felt strongly that this research was
important.
A PPI panel will guide the research to make sure the project responds to the needs and preferences of people
having blood tests. The panel will meet three times a year to review project plans at each stage and provide input
on data analysis. The panel will also write public summaries of the research.
Panel members will be paid for their contributions. They will receive funding for training and attending conferences
to share the research. Two lay people will co-chair a separate Project Advisory Group to steer the project.
Sharing the research
I will share the research with local community groups, patients and GP practice staff through social media, blogs, a
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
Background
The use of blood tests in primary care has increased significantly: testing rates have tripled since 2000, and nearly
half of all NHS tests are now conducted in this setting. While valuable, blood tests are often over-ordered, leading
to high costs (£1.8 billion in 2015-16), increased clinician workload, and potential harm to patients. These harms
include anxiety, undue reassurance, and unwarranted further investigations.
Communicating effectively about the limitations, risks, and purpose of blood tests is critical, yet research shows
that blood test communication is often inadequate. Clinicians rarely explain the purpose or limitations of tests,
while patients often report feeling excluded from testing decisions and unsure about the implications of their
results. NHS guidelines for optimising blood testing in primary care focus primarily on test appropriateness, with
little attention to how tests are discussed with patients.
Understanding what happens in consultations about blood tests is important for addressing the rise in testing and
ensuring that blood tests are used wisely. With patients now able to access blood test results online, these
communication issues warra

nt urgent attention.
Aims
This research will use an ethnographic approach to explore how and whether blood test uncertainty, overuse and
overdiagnosis are discussed in primary care settings. It will employ participatory methods to identify how these
issues could be better communicated.
Research questions
1. How is communication about blood testing in primary care settings framed in the literature?
2. How, where and when do issues like uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis emerge in primary care
interactions about blood testing?
3. What challenges do clinicians face in communicating about blood tests, and how can video-reflexive
ethnography help clinicians to identify solutions to these challenges?
4. How can participatory performance methods engage patients and clinicians to identify more effective ways
of discussing issues with blood tests such as uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis?
Methods
A multi-method study across four work packages (WPs):
 WP1: Meta-ethnography synthesising existing qualitative literature on primary care blood test
communication.
 WP2: Detailed ethnographic observation in two general practices with contrasting patient populations,
focusing on consultat

evidence.text:
Background
Communicating effectively about blood testing is important for ensuring patient safety and involvement in
healthcare. Blood tests are useful, but do not always improve patient care. Sometimes they can cause harm: for
example, borderline test results may worry patients or lead to unnecessary further tests. This is important because
blood tests have become increasingly common: general practice (GP) testing rates have tripled since 2000.
Previous research has shown that patients often feel unsure about why blood tests are done and what results
mean. GP staff do not always clearly explain the reasons for testing and the limitations of blood tests, and patients
report not having enough opportunity to speak to staff about testing. This means that GP staff and patients may
have different expectations and understandings of blood tests. Understanding what happens in consultations
about blood tests is important for addressing the rise in testing and ensuring tests are used wisely.
Aims
This project will explore how blood tests are discussed between patients and GP staff, aiming to improve
communication about the limitations and risks of blood tests. The goal is for patients to be

evidence.text:
Background
The use of blood tests in primary care has increased significantly: testing rates have tripled since 2000, and nearly
half of all NHS tests are now conducted in this setting. While valuable, blood tests are often over-ordered, leading
to high costs (£1.8 billion in 2015-16), increased clinician workload, and potential harm to patients. These harms
include anxiety, undue reassurance, and unwarranted further investigations.
Communicating effectively about the limitations, risks, and purpose of blood tests is critical, yet research shows
that blood test communication is often inadequate. Clinicians rarely explain the purpose or limitations of tests,
while patients often report feeling excluded from testing decisions and unsure about the implications of their
results. NHS guidelines for optimising blood testing in primary care focus primarily on test appropriateness, with
little attention to how tests are discussed with patients.
Understanding what happens in consultations about blood tests is important for addressing the rise in testing and
ensuring that blood tests are used wisely. With patients now able to access blood test results online, these
communication issues warra

test results.19,20 This project
will offer in-depth insight into how digital access shapes blood test communication.
Wise use of blood testing is important for healthcare sustainability. Primary care clinicians request 45% of NHS
blood tests, costing approximately £1.8bn in 2015-16.4 Unnecessary blood tests can yield avoidable follow-up
costs, including hospital visits and investigations.21 Supporting clinicians and patients to have more candid
conversations about the limitations of blood tests may help address medical overuse in the NHS, enabling cost
savings and reducing healthcare's carbon footprint.22
3. Review of existing evidence
Primary care test requests have increased by 8.7% per year on average since 2000.4 Testing is expensive4 and
contributes significantly to GP workload.6 While blood tests can provide important clinical information, many tests
requested are of little diagnostic value and do not improve patient care. The Why Test study, which reviewed
blood test results for 2572 patients across 57 UK GP practices, found that only 6.2% of tests led to a new or
confirmed diagnosis, and 48.8% did not result in patient reassurance or any change in clinical management.5
Unne

cessary and escalating blood testing exposes patients to the risk of overdiagnosis, wherein patients are
harmed by further investigations or treatments without receiving any benefit.7 The potential harms of blood tests
may be overlooked compared to more invasive diagnostic procedures, but the sheer volume of blood testing
means that these cumulative harms warrant careful consideration. Borderline results are common – in one study
of GP blood tests, nearly three-quarters of patients had at least one test result outside the reference
range.5 These results can lead to unnecessary anxiety, particularly if patients access them via online apps.20
False-positive or borderline results can also lead to ‘investigation momentum’: further testing or invasive
procedures such as biopsies or irradiating scans.8 Alternatively, normal test results in a patient presenting with
symptoms may provide undue reassurance, delay diagnosis, or distract from lifestyle changes.9
Despite their limitations, patients have high expectations of blood tests as diagnostic tools23,24 and may
underestimate their potential harms.25 Patients often request blood tests as a ‘check-up’ 23 despite limited
evidence for the v

evidence.text:
nt urgent attention.
Aims
This research will use an ethnographic approach to explore how and whether blood test uncertainty, overuse and
overdiagnosis are discussed in primary care settings. It will employ participatory methods to identify how these
issues could be better communicated.
Research questions
1. How is communication about blood testing in primary care settings framed in the literature?
2. How, where and when do issues like uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis emerge in primary care
interactions about blood testing?
3. What challenges do clinicians face in communicating about blood tests, and how can video-reflexive
ethnography help clinicians to identify solutions to these challenges?
4. How can participatory performance methods engage patients and clinicians to identify more effective ways
of discussing issues with blood tests such as uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis?
Methods
A multi-method study across four work packages (WPs):
 WP1: Meta-ethnography synthesising existing qualitative literature on primary care blood test
communication.
 WP2: Detailed ethnographic observation in two general practices with contrasting patient populations,
focusing on consultat

ions where blood tests are discussed.
 WP3: Video-reflexive ethnography workshops with primary care staff to identify complex challenges
around blood testing communication as well as opportunities for change.
 WP4: Forum Theatre workshops with members of the public and primary care staff to develop strategies
for improving blood test communication. The findings will inform a co-produced workshop (for clinicians)
and an online resource (for patients) to support better blood testing discussions.
Impact
This participatory research project will highlight the primary care consultation as a key site of opportunity for
prudent blood testing. It will engage primary care staff and the public in co-producing resources to encourage more
effective communication about the limitations of blood tests.
Dissemination
The workshop resource will be shared with primary care staff and GP trainees locally, nationally and
internationally. A version of the workshop will be made available as a publicly-accessible resource for patients.
Research findings will be published in academic journals, presented at conferences, and shared through blogs and
podcasts.

2. How, where and when do issues like uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis emerge in primary care
interactions about blood testing?
3. What challenges do clinicians face in communicating about blood tests, and how can video-reflexive
ethnography help clinicians to identify solutions to these challenges?
4. How can participatory performance methods engage patients and clinicians to identify more effective
ways of discussing issues with blood tests such as uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis?
The research questions will be addressed in four sequential work packages, each with specific objectives as
outlined below.
5. Project plan
Work Package (WP) 1: Synthesising existing qualitative literature on primary care blood testing
discussions.
Objectives
1. Identify when, where and how blood tests are discussed in primary care.

existing literature.
3. Deve

Methods
I will conduct a meta-ethnography of qualitative literature on communication about blood tests in primary care.
Meta-ethnography is a form of interpretive qualitative synthesis involving the re-interpretation of primary data and
themes in published research.34 Unlike other aggregative qualitative synthesis approaches, meta-ethnography
aims to develop new theories about the topic under study.35 This work will therefore build on an existing mixed-
communication.36
methods systematic review of blood test In a preliminary scoping review I identified
approximately 20 papers that provide the level of textual detail necessary to support meta-ethnographic synthesis.
I will follow Noblit and Hare’s seven-step process for meta-ethnography to generate theoretical interpretations
which reflect the qualitative synthesis.37
Search strategy
I will systematically search medical, social science and public health databases, using appropriate search terms
and operator functions with the support of QMUL library staff. I will also search grey literature via thesis
repositories,

Participants in PPI workshops felt it was important to explore blood testing practices in diverse patient groups and

General Practice Fingertips data to identify potential research sites which will be recruited with the support of the
NIHR Research Delivery Network.50

I will undertake observation in each site 2-3 times per week over 4-6 months (approximately 200 hours in total).
This schedule, informed by similar studies,6,43,48 balances the need for deep immersion in the field with the
practical constraints of being a lone researcher.

Justification of Costs
Staff costs
At the time of taking up the award I will be a fully-qualified GP (CCT date August 2025). In discussion with the
finance team I have been placed on the QMUL paygrade (C12, spinal point 20) which most closely matches point
1 on the BMA pre-2003 consultant payscale (https://www.bma.org.uk/pay-and-contracts/pay/consultants-pay-
scales/pay-scales-for-consultants-in-england). The salary requested includes London weighting (as I will be based
in London throughout the fellowship) and the QMUL apprenticeship levy (included with London weighting in "other
allowances").
Travel and subsistence
The research will be based in London, where I live, so direct travel costs are minimal. I have included funding to
travel to Bristol twice a year to meet my supervisor, Dr Watson, in person. Many of the courses included in the
training plan are in London, so no tra

duration, accommodation costs would be <£100/night.
Equipment
I have requested funding for a digital voice recorder (for recording ethnographic interviews) and a video camera
(for recording consultations). If I am able to I will borrow these from QMUL, but the length of time I will be spending

People who have recently had blood tests will be involved throughout the project. Nineteen public contributors with
diverse ages, ethnic backgrounds, and health experiences helped to shape the project over three workshops.
They described many issues with communication about blood tests and felt strongly that this research was
important.
A PPI panel will guide the research to make sure the project responds to the needs and preferences of people
having blood tests. The panel will meet three times a year to review project plans at each stage and provide input
on data analysis. The panel will also write public summaries of the research.
Panel members will be paid for their contributions. They will receive funding for training and attending conferences

Aims
This research will use an ethnographic approach to explore how and whether blood test uncertainty, overuse and
overdiagnosis are discussed in primary care settings. It will employ participatory methods to identify how these
issues could be better communicated.
Research questions
1. How is communication about blood testing in primary care settings framed in the literature?
2. How, where and when do issues like uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis emerge in primary care
interactions about blood testing?
3. What challenges do clinicians face in communicating about blood tests, and how can video-reflexive
ethnography help clinicians to identify solutions to these challenges?
4. How can participatory performance methods engage patients and clinicians to identify more effective ways
of discussing issues with blood tests such as uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis?
Methods
A multi-method study across four work packages (WPs):
 WP1: Meta-ethnography synthesising existing qualitative literature on primary care blood test
communication.
 WP2: Detailed ethnographic observation in two general practices with contrasting patient populations,
focusing on consultat

s, and interactions.45,48,49
Sampling
Participants in PPI workshops felt it was important to explore blood testing practices in diverse patient groups and
across different geographical settings. I will therefore purposively select two contrasting GP practices: an inner-
city practice with a young, multi-ethnic population, and a suburban practice with an older population. I will use
General Practice Fingertips data to identify potential research sites which will be recruited with the support of the
NIHR Research Delivery Network.50
Data collection
I will undertake observation in each site 2-3 times per week over 4-6 months (approximately 200 hours in total).
This schedule, informed by similar studies,6,43,48 balances the need for deep immersion in the field with the
practical constraints of being a lone researcher.
Data collection will include:
1. Observation of professional practices
I will observe clinical consultations, reception/administrative areas, interprofessional meetings,
o
and Patient Participant Groups. Only half of primary care blood tests are ordered by GPs,5 so I
will also observe pharmacists, nurses and phlebotomists to ensure the dataset reflects the
diversity of pr

imary care blood testing interactions. I will make detailed fieldnotes during and after
observations.
2. Ethnographic interviews with patients and GP staff
o I will conduct informal ad-hoc interviews with patients and GP staff, which will be audio-recorded
(with consent) or included in fieldnotes.
o To explore digital communication about blood tests, I will conduct ‘think-aloud’ interviews, inviting
patients and staff to describe their thoughts as they use digital resources like the NHS app.6,51
3. Gathering written communication relating to blood testing
o I will gather and analyse relevant written materials, e.g. consultation notes, screen captures from
patient apps.
4. Recording consultations
o I will record approximately 45 consultations/’think-aloud’ interviews covering key stages of the
blood testing process, e.g.:
a. discussing/ordering tests;
b. receiving/filing blood test results;
c. communicating results to patients.
Dataset diversity
I will ensure that a range of primary care clinicians are represented in the recordings, and will include
consultations with patients of different ages, sexes and ethnicities, as well as those with multimorbidity and mental
illness.
I will a

lso record consultations involving interpreters and patient advocates/carers. I have made provision in the
budget for patient information and consent forms to be translated into the three most common patient languages
(besides English) in each research site.
Consent
I will seek consent for recording from patients and clinicians either prospectively (for face-to-face consultations) or
retrospectively (e.g. if seeking to access audio consultation data from practice telephone systems). Participants in
PPI workshops were surprised that many GP practices automatically recorded telephone consultations and felt
patients needed to be told this before being approached for consent. I will consider this in the consent procedure,
with input from the PPI panel and research sites. Clinicians and patients will be able to withdraw their consent for
recording during the consultation or request that the recorded data be destroyed at any point afterwards.
I discuss the feasibility of recording further in Section 9.

1. Elicit the perspectives of primary care staff on the challenges of communicating about bl

evidence.text:
Background
Communicating effectively about blood testing is important for ensuring patient safety and involvement in
healthcare. Blood tests are useful, but do not always improve patient care. Sometimes they can cause harm: for
example, borderline test results may worry patients or lead to unnecessary further tests. This is important because
blood tests have become increasingly common: general practice (GP) testing rates have tripled since 2000.
Previous research has shown that patients often feel unsure about why blood tests are done and what results
mean. GP staff do not always clearly explain the reasons for testing and the limitations of blood tests, and patients
report not having enough opportunity to speak to staff about testing. This means that GP staff and patients may
have different expectations and understandings of blood tests. Understanding what happens in consultations
about blood tests is important for addressing the rise in testing and ensuring tests are used wisely.
Aims
This project will explore how blood tests are discussed between patients and GP staff, aiming to improve
communication about the limitations and risks of blood tests. The goal is for patients to be

evidence.text:
Background
The use of blood tests in primary care has increased significantly: testing rates have tripled since 2000, and nearly
half of all NHS tests are now conducted in this setting. While valuable, blood tests are often over-ordered, leading
to high costs (£1.8 billion in 2015-16), increased clinician workload, and potential harm to patients. These harms
include anxiety, undue reassurance, and unwarranted further investigations.
Communicating effectively about the limitations, risks, and purpose of blood tests is critical, yet research shows
that blood test communication is often inadequate. Clinicians rarely explain the purpose or limitations of tests,
while patients often report feeling excluded from testing decisions and unsure about the implications of their
results. NHS guidelines for optimising blood testing in primary care focus primarily on test appropriateness, with
little attention to how tests are discussed with patients.
Understanding what happens in consultations about blood tests is important for addressing the rise in testing and
ensuring that blood tests are used wisely. With patients now able to access blood test results online, these
communication issues warra

nt urgent attention.
Aims
This research will use an ethnographic approach to explore how and whether blood test uncertainty, overuse and
overdiagnosis are discussed in primary care settings. It will employ participatory methods to identify how these
issues could be better communicated.
Research questions
1. How is communication about blood testing in primary care settings framed in the literature?
2. How, where and when do issues like uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis emerge in primary care
interactions about blood testing?
3. What challenges do clinicians face in communicating about blood tests, and how can video-reflexive
ethnography help clinicians to identify solutions to these challenges?
4. How can participatory performance methods engage patients and clinicians to identify more effective ways
of discussing issues with blood tests such as uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis?
Methods
A multi-method study across four work packages (WPs):
 WP1: Meta-ethnography synthesising existing qualitative literature on primary care blood test
communication.
 WP2: Detailed ethnographic observation in two general practices with contrasting patient populations,
focusing on consultat

evidence.text:
Background
The use of blood tests in primary care has increased significantly: testing rates have tripled since 2000, and nearly
half of all NHS tests are now conducted in this setting. While valuable, blood tests are often over-ordered, leading
to high costs (£1.8 billion in 2015-16), increased clinician workload, and potential harm to patients. These harms
include anxiety, undue reassurance, and unwarranted further investigations.
Communicating effectively about the limitations, risks, and purpose of blood tests is critical, yet research shows
that blood test communication is often inadequate. Clinicians rarely explain the purpose or limitations of tests,
while patients often report feeling excluded from testing decisions and unsure about the implications of their
results. NHS guidelines for optimising blood testing in primary care focus primarily on test appropriateness, with
little attention to how tests are discussed with patients.
Understanding what happens in consultations about blood tests is important for addressing the rise in testing and
ensuring that blood tests are used wisely. With patients now able to access blood test results online, these
communication issues warra

test results.19,20 This project
will offer in-depth insight into how digital access shapes blood test communication.
Wise use of blood testing is important for healthcare sustainability. Primary care clinicians request 45% of NHS
blood tests, costing approximately £1.8bn in 2015-16.4 Unnecessary blood tests can yield avoidable follow-up
costs, including hospital visits and investigations.21 Supporting clinicians and patients to have more candid
conversations about the limitations of blood tests may help address medical overuse in the NHS, enabling cost
savings and reducing healthcare's carbon footprint.22
3. Review of existing evidence
Primary care test requests have increased by 8.7% per year on average since 2000.4 Testing is expensive4 and
contributes significantly to GP workload.6 While blood tests can provide important clinical information, many tests
requested are of little diagnostic value and do not improve patient care. The Why Test study, which reviewed
blood test results for 2572 patients across 57 UK GP practices, found that only 6.2% of tests led to a new or
confirmed diagnosis, and 48.8% did not result in patient reassurance or any change in clinical management.5
Unne

cessary and escalating blood testing exposes patients to the risk of overdiagnosis, wherein patients are
harmed by further investigations or treatments without receiving any benefit.7 The potential harms of blood tests
may be overlooked compared to more invasive diagnostic procedures, but the sheer volume of blood testing
means that these cumulative harms warrant careful consideration. Borderline results are common – in one study
of GP blood tests, nearly three-quarters of patients had at least one test result outside the reference
range.5 These results can lead to unnecessary anxiety, particularly if patients access them via online apps.20
False-positive or borderline results can also lead to ‘investigation momentum’: further testing or invasive
procedures such as biopsies or irradiating scans.8 Alternatively, normal test results in a patient presenting with
symptoms may provide undue reassurance, delay diagnosis, or distract from lifestyle changes.9
Despite their limitations, patients have high expectations of blood tests as diagnostic tools23,24 and may
underestimate their potential harms.25 Patients often request blood tests as a ‘check-up’ 23 despite limited
evidence for the v

Aims
This research will use an ethnographic approach to explore how and whether blood test uncertainty, overuse and
overdiagnosis are discussed in primary care settings. It will employ participatory methods to identify how these
issues could be better communicated.
Research questions
1. How is communication about blood testing in primary care settings framed in the literature?
2. How, where and when do issues like uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis emerge in primary care
interactions about blood testing?
3. What challenges do clinicians face in communicating about blood tests, and how can video-reflexive
ethnography help clinicians to identify solutions to these challenges?
4. How can participatory performance methods engage patients and clinicians to identify more effective ways
of discussing issues with blood tests such as uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis?
Methods
A multi-method study across four work packages (WPs):
 WP1: Meta-ethnography synthesising existing qualitative literature on primary care blood test
communication.
 WP2: Detailed ethnographic observation in two general practices with contrasting patient populations,
focusing on consultat

ions where blood tests are discussed.
 WP3: Video-reflexive ethnography workshops with primary care staff to identify complex challenges
around blood testing communication as well as opportunities for change.
 WP4: Forum Theatre workshops with members of the public and primary care staff to develop strategies
for improving blood test communication. The findings will inform a co-produced workshop (for clinicians)
and an online resource (for patients) to support better blood testing discussions.
Impact
This participatory research project will highlight the primary care consultation as a key site of opportunity for
prudent blood testing. It will engage primary care staff and the public in co-producing resources to encourage more
effective communication about the limitations of blood tests.
Dissemination
The workshop resource will be shared with primary care staff and GP trainees locally, nationally and
internationally. A version of the workshop will be made available as a publicly-accessible resource for patients.
Research findings will be published in academic journals, presented at conferences, and shared through blogs and
podcasts.

1. How is communication about blood testing in primary care settings framed in the literature?
2. How, where and when do issues like uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis emerge in primary care
interactions about blood testing?
3. What challenges do clinicians face in communicating about blood tests, and how can video-reflexive
ethnography help clinicians to identify solutions to these challenges?
4. How can participatory performance methods engage patients and clinicians to identify more effective
ways of discussing issues with blood tests such as uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis?
The research questions will be addressed in four sequential work packages, each with specific objectives as
outlined below.
5. Project plan
Work Package (WP) 1: Synthesising existing qualitative literature on primary care blood testing
discussions.
Objectives
1. Identify when, where and how blood tests are discussed in primary care.
2. Explore how patient and clinician perspectives on and experiences of blood testing are framed in the
existing literature.
3. Deve

lop new conceptual understandings of blood test communication in primary care settings to inform
later work packages.
Methods
I will conduct a meta-ethnography of qualitative literature on communication about blood tests in primary care.
Meta-ethnography is a form of interpretive qualitative synthesis involving the re-interpretation of primary data and
themes in published research.34 Unlike other aggregative qualitative synthesis approaches, meta-ethnography
aims to develop new theories about the topic under study.35 This work will therefore build on an existing mixed-
communication.36
methods systematic review of blood test In a preliminary scoping review I identified
approximately 20 papers that provide the level of textual detail necessary to support meta-ethnographic synthesis.
I will follow Noblit and Hare’s seven-step process for meta-ethnography to generate theoretical interpretations
which reflect the qualitative synthesis.37
Search strategy
I will systematically search medical, social science and public health databases, using appropriate search terms
and operator functions with the support of QMUL library staff. I will also search grey literature via thesis
repositories,

ions where blood tests are discussed.

Methods
I will conduct a meta-ethnography of qualitative literature on communication about blood tests in primary care.
Meta-ethnography is a form of interpretive qualitative synthesis involving the re-interpretation of primary data and
themes in published research.34 Unlike other aggregative qualitative synthesis approaches, meta-ethnography
aims to develop new theories about the topic under study.35 This work will therefore build on an existing mixed-
communication.36
methods systematic review of blood test In a preliminary scoping review I identified
approximately 20 papers that provide the level of textual detail necessary to support meta-ethnographic synthesis.
I will follow Noblit and Hare’s seven-step process for meta-ethnography to generate theoretical interpretations
which reflect the qualitative synthesis.37
Search strategy
I will systematically search medical, social science and public health databases, using appropriate search terms
and operator functions with the support of QMUL library staff. I will also search grey literature via thesis
repositories,

evidence.text:
Background
Communicating effectively about blood testing is important for ensuring patient safety and involvement in
healthcare. Blood tests are useful, but do not always improve patient care. Sometimes they can cause harm: for
example, borderline test results may worry patients or lead to unnecessary further tests. This is important because
blood tests have become increasingly common: general practice (GP) testing rates have tripled since 2000.
Previous research has shown that patients often feel unsure about why blood tests are done and what results
mean. GP staff do not always clearly explain the reasons for testing and the limitations of blood tests, and patients
report not having enough opportunity to speak to staff about testing. This means that GP staff and patients may
have different expectations and understandings of blood tests. Understanding what happens in consultations
about blood tests is important for addressing the rise in testing and ensuring tests are used wisely.
Aims
This project will explore how blood tests are discussed between patients and GP staff, aiming to improve
communication about the limitations and risks of blood tests. The goal is for patients to be
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
more involved in
decisions about blood testing and understand better what blood tests mean for their health.
Research design
The project has four parts:
1. Evidence search: I will search and review existing research on how blood tests are discussed in general
practices. This will help to understand what is already known and what is missing.
2. Observation at GP practices: I will spend several months watching conversations about blood tests at two
different general practices. I will make recordings of patient consultations with GP staff (including GPs,
nurses, pharmacists and phlebotomists) to look at how blood tests are discussed.
3. Workshops with GP staff: I will show short recordings of blood test discussions to staff. They will consider
how blood tests are discussed with patients and think about how communication can be improved.
4. Theatre workshops with the public: Professional actors and public contributors will create a short play
based on the research. This will be performed to members of the public as part of “Forum Theatre”
workshops. Workshop attendees will be invited to make changes to the play to improve communication
about blood tests. Public contributors will help t

The finance section should provide a breakdown of costs associated with undertaking the research as
described in the proposal.
