Staff type Lead Applicant
Name Lotte Elton
Employing
Queen Mary University of London
organisation
Grade Clinical Academic (C12)
Starting point on scale 20
Increment Date 01/10/2026
Justification of Costs
Staff costs
At the time of taking up the award I will be a fully-qualified GP (CCT date August 2025). In discussion with the
finance team I have been placed on the QMUL paygrade (C12, spinal point 20) which most closely matches point
1 on the BMA pre-2003 consultant payscale (https://www.bma.org.uk/pay-and-contracts/pay/consultants-pay-
scales/pay-scales-for-consultants-in-england). The salary requested includes London weighting (as I will be based
in London throughout the fellowship) and the QMUL apprenticeship levy (included with London weighting in "other
allowances").
Travel and subsistence
The research will be based in London, where I live, so direct travel costs are minimal. I have included funding to
travel to Bristol twice a year to meet my supervisor, Dr Watson, in person. Many of the courses included in the
training plan are in London, so no tra

duration, accommodation costs would be <£100/night.
Equipment
I have requested funding for a digital voice recorder (for recording ethnographic interviews) and a video camera
(for recording consultations). If I am able to I will borrow these from QMUL, but the length of time I will be spending
in the field means that it may not be practical to use shared university equipment. I will need an encrypted hard
drive to store this data and have included funding for a second back-up hard drive to prevent loss of data if the
principal hard drive breaks or is lost. On the advice of my supervisors, who have done video-reflexive ethnography
workshops before, I have included funding for a video projector. This can be used when showing video clips during
video-reflexive ethnography workshops and avoids any potential difficulties using encrypted hard drives on NHS
computers.
Consumables
These costs include funding for printing consent forms and information leaflets/flyers. These will be distributed in
research sites to introduce the research as well as in local GP practices to share the research findings.
PPI
I have embedded robust Patient and Public Involvement throughout the proposal. Public c

workshops, I have included funding for Social Action for
Health, a community health charity in East London which has worked closely with QMUL researchers for several
years. They usually charge approximately £2,000 per focus group (with translators) so the £5,000 fee should cover
two Forum Theatre workshops. They have longstanding links within the local community, including with people not
usually reached by research (e.g. those who do not speak English as a first language). I will liaise with Social
Action for Health to ensure that the Forum Theatre workshops are as representative of the local community as
possible.
Dissemination costs
The fees paid to actors and to Improbable (who will help in recruiting actors and can also help to publicise the
resources) will ensure that the Forum Theatre workshops are conducted by trained professionals with experience
in improvisation and participatory theatre. I have been advised by Improbable to cost in £4,000 to compensate
them for their staff members’ time. I have set actors’ fees at £300/day, roughly in line with Equity (the actors’
union) industry standards for independent productions.

creening to patients (top 5% of all Altmetric-scored outputs). I was invited to present this
work at Preventing Overdiagnosis 2022.
I then completed a Diploma in Philosophy of Medicine, exploring in my dissertation how clinicians balance risk
when diagnosing patients as dying. This was awarded the Sydney Selwyn prize for the best Diploma dissertation. I
was invited to give a public lecture on my research and published it as a single-author paper in Journal of
Evaluation in Clinical Practice (2022). I presented this work at Preventing Overdiagnosis 2023.
I belong to the Preventing Overdiagnosis Early Career Researchers group and am part of the pre-conference
organising committee for the 2025 conference.
NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship, King's College London (2021-Aug 2025)
I have been a Research Fellow on Dr Andy Guise’s UKRI-funded Social Responses to Stigma project since
beginning my academic placement in 2023. This ethnographic study examines how stigma and discrimination
arise within the health and care services used by people who are homeless in south London. My work includes
ethnographic observation in day centres and collaborative analysis of fieldnotes and interviews. I le

ad a research
strand exploring intersections between stigma, homelessness, and health self-management.
Outputs include:
 Presentation at the RCGP Conference 2024.
 First-author paper in preparation (for submission to Sociology of Health and Illness).
 Co-author on a quantitative survey of homeless people’s stigma experiences, (under review, BMJ Open).
 Co-author of a forthcoming submission on drug use stigma (International Journal of Drug Policy).
In 2022, I collaborated with researchers at Charité University Berlin and Medical University of Vienna on a project
to improve Type 2 diabetes prevention in women with gestational diabetes. We secured a £10,000 European
University Hospitals Alliance grant and convened workshops in London, Berlin and Vienna. I am first author of our
interdisciplinary commentary paper (provisionally accepted January 2025, Journal of Midwifery and Women’s
Health).
Other activities:
 PGCert in Applied Research Methods (KCL) (completion June 2025). Awarded Distinction in all completed
modules.
 Advisory board member (2022-2024) for PREPARED, an interventional primary care study to improve
pregnancy outcomes in women with diabetes.
 Invited peer editor f

or Professor David Misselbrook’s textbook, Thinking About Medicine: An Introduction to
the Philosophy of Medicine.
 Peer reviewer for Social Science and Medicine, British Journal of General Practice, Journal of Medical
Ethics, BMC Medical Ethics, Digital Health, and Information, Communication & Society.
MPhil, University of Cambridge (2019-2020)
My MPhil (First Class with Distinction) combined medical philosophy, sociology and anthropology. My thesis
(Starred Distinction) was a qualitative interview study exploring how women’s experience of pregnancy was
shaped by participating in an online gestational diabetes support community. I published this work as sole author
in Sociology of Health and Illness (2022) and presented it at three international conferences, including the
International Federation of Gynaecology and Obstetrics World Congress (2021).
Previous research experience
Honorary Research Fellow, Queen Mary University of London (2017-2019):
 Led a systematic review of post-partum depression and intimate partner violence in high-income countries
– awarded 1st prize at the British Society for Biopsychosocial Obstetrics and Gynaecology conference
(2018).

4. How can participatory performance methods engage patients and clinicians to identify more effective ways
of discussing issues with blood tests such as uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis?

A multi-method study across four work packages (WPs):
 WP1: Meta-ethnography synthesising existing qualitative literature on primary care blood test
communication.
 WP2: Detailed ethnographic observation in two general practices with contrasting patient populations,
focusing on consultat

s, and interactions.45,48,49
Sampling
Participants in PPI workshops felt it was important to explore blood testing practices in diverse patient groups and
across different geographical settings. I will therefore purposively select two contrasting GP practices: an inner-
city practice with a young, multi-ethnic population, and a suburban practice with an older population. I will use
General Practice Fingertips data to identify potential research sites which will be recruited with the support of the
NIHR Research Delivery Network.50
Data collection
I will undertake observation in each site 2-3 times per week over 4-6 months (approximately 200 hours in total).
This schedule, informed by similar studies,6,43,48 balances the need for deep immersion in the field with the
practical constraints of being a lone researcher.
Data collection will include:
1. Observation of professional practices
I will observe clinical consultations, reception/administrative areas, interprofessional meetings,
o
and Patient Participant Groups. Only half of primary care blood tests are ordered by GPs,5 so I
will also observe pharmacists, nurses and phlebotomists to ensure the dataset reflects the
diversity of pr

imary care blood testing interactions. I will make detailed fieldnotes during and after
observations.
2. Ethnographic interviews with patients and GP staff
o I will conduct informal ad-hoc interviews with patients and GP staff, which will be audio-recorded
(with consent) or included in fieldnotes.
o To explore digital communication about blood tests, I will conduct ‘think-aloud’ interviews, inviting
patients and staff to describe their thoughts as they use digital resources like the NHS app.6,51
3. Gathering written communication relating to blood testing
o I will gather and analyse relevant written materials, e.g. consultation notes, screen captures from
patient apps.
4. Recording consultations
o I will record approximately 45 consultations/’think-aloud’ interviews covering key stages of the
blood testing process, e.g.:
a. discussing/ordering tests;
b. receiving/filing blood test results;
c. communicating results to patients.
Dataset diversity
I will ensure that a range of primary care clinicians are represented in the recordings, and will include
consultations with patients of different ages, sexes and ethnicities, as well as those with multimorbidity and mental
illness.
I will a

lso record consultations involving interpreters and patient advocates/carers. I have made provision in the
budget for patient information and consent forms to be translated into the three most common patient languages
(besides English) in each research site.
Consent
I will seek consent for recording from patients and clinicians either prospectively (for face-to-face consultations) or
retrospectively (e.g. if seeking to access audio consultation data from practice telephone systems). Participants in
PPI workshops were surprised that many GP practices automatically recorded telephone consultations and felt
patients needed to be told this before being approached for consent. I will consider this in the consent procedure,
with input from the PPI panel and research sites. Clinicians and patients will be able to withdraw their consent for
recording during the consultation or request that the recorded data be destroyed at any point afterwards.
I discuss the feasibility of recording further in Section 9.

The QMUL Doctoral College and the Wolfson Institute of Population Health offer doctoral development

evidence.text:
velop new collaborations:
At QMUL:
 APOLLO Social Sciences team:
o Multidisciplinary team of qualitative primary care researchers, led by Professor Swinglehurst.
Alongside regular team meetings I will have the opportunity to attend APOLLO’s termly writing
retreat.
 Thinking Between the Lines:
o Qualitative research network, co-convened by Dr Fudge, holding monthly meetings for
researchers to present work-in-progress and receive feedback.
 Curiosity Workshops:
Monthly gatherings to share and seek advice on qualitative research in-progress, led by Professor
o
Swinglehurst.
Externally:
 Preventing Overdiagnosis Early/Mid-Career Researcher Network:
o International network of researchers working on overdiagnosis, which provided helpful feedback
on this proposal.
 RCGP Optimal Testing group:
o Subgroup of the RCGP Overdiagnosis interest group, led by Dr Watson. I will attend their tri-yearly
meetings to share my work and discuss how to improve primary care blood testing practices.
Research visit
In the 3rd year of the PhD, I have been invited to spend six weeks in Australia visiting two research departments.
Both departments are pioneering work on the communication of overdiagnosis
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
to patients. In each site I will be
mentored by the unit lead and a mid-career researcher. We will agree a timetable in advance of my visit.
 Sydney Health Literacy Lab, University of Sydney
The Lab focuses on healthcare communication to prevent overdiagnosis and low-value care. I will
o
spend four weeks here, hosted by Professor Carissa Bonner, a behavioural scientist who
specialises in health literacy and risk communication, and Dr Brooke Nickel.
o I will use insights from the Lab’s extensive work co-producing health literacy tools to aid the
development of the WP4 resource for patients.
 Australian Centre for Health Engagement, Evidence and Values (ACHEEV), University of Wollongong.
o I will spend two weeks at ACHEEV, hosted by Professor Stacy Carter and Dr Patti Shih. The
ACHEEV team aims to find solutions to medical overuse by making evidence more accessible.
I am a technical advisor for Dr Shih’s project developing an online resource to explain blood test
o
results to patients, and will be able to develop this collaboration during my visit.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 1

Institution Queen Mary, University of London

criteria.name: Quality and Relevance of Proposed Training
criteria.definition: How well-structured, clear, and relevant the training plan is in relation to the project’s goals and the applicant’s research needs.

signals.signal_text: Completeness and clarity of training description
Alignment of training topics with project methods or techniques
Structured timeline or sequencing of training activities
Explicit links between training content and skill acquisition

criteria.pros: The application provides a highly detailed, itemized training plan covering specific courses (e.g., Advanced Qualitative Research Methods, Forum Theatre Intensive Training) with providers, durations, and costs. There is a clear alignment between training topics (meta-ethnography, ethnography, creative co-production) and the project's work packages (WP1-WP4). The plan explicitly links training content to skill acquisition (e.g., 'underpin the qualitative methods training', 'support me in the close analysis').
criteria.drawbacks: While the training plan is comprehensive, the specific sequencing or timeline for when each course will be taken relative to the project phases is implied rather than explicitly mapped to a Gantt chart or specific months in the text, though the budget section does allocate costs by year.
criteria.rationale: While the training plan is comprehensive, the specific sequencing or timeline for when each course will be taken relative to the project phases is implied rather than explicitly mapped to a Gantt chart or specific months in the text, though the budget section does allocate costs by year.

Connections between training activities and future career trajectory
Indications of skill transferability beyond the current project
Reference to professional practice or experiential learning where applicable

criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly states career goals ('continue my development as a clinical researcher', 'independent research leader'). Training activities are directly connected to this trajectory, such as the 'Emerging Clinical Leaders' programme for leadership skills and 'Knowledge into Action' to inform postdoctoral funding applications. The plan includes transferable skills like science communication, website design, and leadership, which extend beyond the immediate project. Experiential learning is well-supported through a 6-week research visit to Australia and mentorship from established leaders.
criteria.drawbacks: No material drawbacks; the evidence is complete and specific.
criteria.rationale: No material drawbacks; the evidence is complete and specific.

Dissemination and impact
Patient and public involvement/engagement:
To support me in working with public contributors throughout the project I will take the following courses:
 Co-design, Co-production and Participatory Action Research; Inclusivity within Research Processes (1 day
each, University of Bristol)
 Hands on Engagement: Taking Research to Events and Festivals (1 day, QMUL): course to prepare me to
host the dissemination event.
Implementation of findings:
 Knowledge into Action (8 weeks part-time, University of Oxford, £3175), led by Professor Carl Heneghan.
This module will support my plans for impact and dissemination, helping me to understand how my
recommendations can be implemented in clinical practice. It will also inform an application for postdoctoral
funding to develop and evaluate interventions to improve blood test communication.
Disseminating findings:
 Science Communication Masterclass (4 days, UWE Bristol, £400) : intensive course on communicating
research findings

The Lab focuses on healthcare communication to prevent overdiagnosis and low-value care. I will
o
spend four weeks here, hosted by Professor Carissa Bonner, a behavioural scientist who

o I will use insights from the Lab’s extensive work co-producing health literacy tools to aid the
development of the WP4 resource for patients.
 Australian Centre for Health Engagement, Evidence and Values (ACHEEV), University of Wollongong.
o I will spend two weeks at ACHEEV, hosted by Professor Stacy Carter and Dr Patti Shih. The
ACHEEV team aims to find solutions to medical overuse by making evidence more accessible.
I am a technical advisor for Dr Shih’s project developing an online resource to explain blood test
o
results to patients, and will be able to develop this collaboration during my visit.

evidence.text:
Primary Care
Description of support provided
Deborah Swinglehurst is a Professor of Primary Care and general practitioner. Her academic work uses qualitative
methods, including ethnography and linguistic ethnography, to explore how interaction and social relationships
shape healthcare practices. She leads the APOLLO social science group, adopting innovative methodologies at
the interfaces of medicine, social science and linguistics to address complex healthcare challenges.
She is Institute co-lead for Applied Social Science and sits on the Executive Board and Research Committee. She
is in the senior management team for the Wellcome-funded Doctoral Training Programme on Health Data in
Practice and the leadership team for QMUL’s NIHR SPCR membership.
Professor Swinglehurst has published widely in journals including BMJ, BMJ Quality & Safety, BJGP, and Social
Science and Medicine. She is on the 2024 Stanford/Elsevier Top 2% Scientists list of the most cited scientists
globally and was awarded the 2019 RCGP/SAPC John Fry Award for her contribution to general practice. Her
recent NIHR-funded research on multimorbidity and polypharmacy involved co-producing a suite of RCGP e-
learning mo
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
dules (reaching >1000 learners) and a public engagement resource (Let’s Talk Differently about
Medicines) which has reached >1800 people.
She will provide expertise in meta-ethnography, ethnographic approaches (including linguistic ethnography/video-
reflexive ethnography), and creative co-production. We will have supervision meetings fortnightly and she will
provide further support as needed.
Accredited by the UK Council for Graduate Education as a recognised research supervisor, she has supervised 6
PhD students to completion, currently supervises 6 PhD students, and has examined 10 PhDs.

Institution University of Bristol
Position NIHR Academic Clinical Lecturer in General Practice
Description of support provided
Jessica Watson is a general practitioner and NIHR Academic Clinical Lecturer whose research uses mixed
methods to understand and improve primary care blood testing practices. She chairs the Primary Care Academic
Collaborative (PACT) and leads the Optimal Testing division of the RCGP Overdiagnosis group.
Dr Watson has been awarded three consecutive NIHR Fellowships and has delivered over £2m of research
funding. She h

The QMUL Centre for Academic and Professional Development provides a comprehensive programme of short
courses and bespoke support. These include writing and presentation skills, personal organisation, academic
skills, career planning, and media training. The Centre for Public Engagement also provides funding to support
public engagement and hosts the annual ‘Festival of Communities’ event in east London.

sub_criteria.definition: Quality, clarity, and appropriateness of supervision and mentoring for supporting the applicant’s research and career development.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of supervisor roles and responsibilities
Evidence of mentorship plans, meetings, or training support
Alignment between supervisor expertise and project needs
Presence of structured supervision framework (frequency, format, accountability)

sub_criteria.pros: The application details a robust and structured supervisory framework. It explicitly describes fortnightly/monthly meetings and the establishment of a Project Advisory Group comprising experts in overdiagnosis, ethics, and applied theatre. The budget explicitly allocates funds for twice-yearly travel to Bristol for supervisory meetings, providing financial support for this structured framework. The alignment between supervisor expertise and project needs is clear.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks identified; the supervision plan is comprehensive and well-resourced.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks identified; the supervision plan is comprehensive and well-resourced.

od tests, feel more confident in making shared decisions
about testing, and have a clearer grasp of the implications of their results.
For primary care staff: They will gain confidence in explaining the risks and limitations of blood tests, improving
their ability to discuss testing decisions and results with patients.
For the NHS: Better communication and shared decision-making could improve patient safety and reduce
unnecessary testing. This will reduce blood testing costs, ease administrative workloads, and reduce the costs
and carbon footprint associated with repeat/follow-up investigations.
7. Project management

team. I have uploaded a detailed research timetable.
Progress will be monitored through:
Supervision
Fortnightly meetings with Professor Swinglehurst.

 Email support and feedback on written work from all supervisors as required.

Formal reviews
As per QMUL regulations, I will have yearly reviews to assess progress and identify additional support/training
needs.
8. Ethics
I will obtain ethical approval from QMUL and the NHS Health Research Authority before starting any data
collection involving participants. Key ethical considerations include:
Ethnographic observation and recording
I will get written consent from all GP practice staff for observation. My presence will be respectful and unobtrusive
and I will leave a space immediately if asked. GP staff will seek a patient’s explicit consent for me to observe their
care, ideally without me present (e.g. when collecting patients from the waiting room).69 I will clearly explain my
dual role as a researcher and practising GP.
Video-reflexive ethnography workshops
Staff may feel uncomfortable watching or hearing comments about their consultations. I will mitigate this by
ensuring that staff are aware of the purpose of the workshops: i.e., to collectively identify new ways of working,

sub_criteria.definition: Evidence that the organisation fosters inclusivity, research integrity, and a supportive environment for all researchers.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of policies or practices promoting diversity, equity, and inclusion
Evidence of inclusive recruitment, mentoring, or support initiatives
References to adherence to research integrity principles
Statements on organisational culture, training, or initiatives supporting underrepresented groups

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides strong evidence of a commitment to an inclusive research culture. It details QMUL's SWAN Gold award, its diverse student body, and embedded EDI leadership structures. The text explicitly references adherence to research integrity principles and initiatives supporting underrepresented groups, creating a supportive environment for the applicant.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the institutional policies are well-documented, specific examples of how these policies have directly supported previous fellows in this specific department could strengthen the evidence further, though the current evidence is sufficient for a high score.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the institutional policies are well-documented, specific examples of how these policies have directly supported previous fellows in this specific department could strengthen the evidence further, though the current evidence is sufficient for a high score.

WIPH currently hosts 130 postgraduate research students. This includes two Wellcome-funded Doctoral Training
Programmes (Health Data in Practice (HDIP)); Primary Care Clinicians). Prof. Swinglehurst is co-director of the
HDIP programme and Dr Fudge is an Associate Director. The Institute also recruits doctoral students via the Trials
Methodology Research Partnership DTP, the School for Primary Care Research PhD studentship scheme, the
London Interdisciplinary Social Sciences DTP, the City of London Cancer Centre PhD studentships, and other
national and international schemes. Lotte will therefore be well-supported by a network of peers and mentors.
Academic support for WIPH PGRs is led by our excellent Graduate Studies team (Dr Fudge is Deputy Director)
and our Graduate School offers induction and ongoing support including personal development workshops,
wellbeing and counselling, travel awards, training events.

of outputs ranked 3*-4* in both environment and impact case studies.
QMUL is one of the most ethnically diverse research-led universities in the world. Commitment to equality,
diversity and inclusion (EDI) is embedded into all day-to-day and strategic decision-making within the Faculty,
reflected in our research aims (‘Every life is precious’). This work is overseen by six staff leads, who coordinate a
network of academic and professional services staff with recognised roles within each institute. We proactively
embed EDI into all meeting structures, policies and procedures. Faculty and institute-level leadership committees
include EDI representation, and institute EDI reps ensure that faculty-level initiatives result in meaningful outcomes
as well as developing and leading local change. FMD was the first QMUL faculty to receive a SWAN Bronze award
(2008) and recently achieved Gold (2024).
FMD has a Flexible Research Fund scheme through which 20% of overheads earned on any FEC grant/award is
returned to the investigator. Lotte would be able to use this for approved research-related activity (e.g. training,

Research design
The project has four parts:
1. Evidence search: I will search and review existing research on how blood tests are discussed in general
practices. This will help to understand what is already known and what is missing.
2. Observation at GP practices: I will spend several months watching conversations about blood tests at two
different general practices. I will make recordings of patient consultations with GP staff (including GPs,
nurses, pharmacists and phlebotomists) to look at how blood tests are discussed.
3. Workshops with GP staff: I will show short recordings of blood test discussions to staff. They will consider
how blood tests are discussed with patients and think about how communication can be improved.
4. Theatre workshops with the public: Professional actors and public contributors will create a short play
based on the research. This will be performed to members of the public as part of “Forum Theatre”
workshops. Workshop attendees will be invited to make changes to the play to improve communication
about blood tests. Public contributors will help t

around blood testing communication as well as opportunities for change.
 WP4: Forum Theatre workshops with members of the public and primary care staff to develop strategies
for improving blood test communication. The findings will inform a co-produced workshop (for clinicians)
and an online resource (for patients) to support better blood testing discussions.
Impact
This participatory research project will highlight the primary care consultation as a key site of opportunity for
prudent blood testing. It will engage primary care staff and the public in co-producing resources to encourage more
effective communication about the limitations of blood tests.
Dissemination
The workshop resource will be shared with primary care staff and GP trainees locally, nationally and
internationally. A version of the workshop will be made available as a publicly-accessible resource for patients.
Research findings will be published in academic journals, presented at conferences, and shared through blogs and
podcasts.

Sampling
Participants in PPI workshops felt it was important to explore blood testing practices in diverse patient groups and
across different geographical settings. I will therefore purposively select two contrasting GP practices: an inner-
city practice with a young, multi-ethnic population, and a suburban practice with an older population. I will use
General Practice Fingertips data to identify potential research sites which will be recruited with the support of the
NIHR Research Delivery Network.50
Data collection

This schedule, informed by similar studies,6,43,48 balances the need for deep immersion in the field with the
practical constraints of being a lone researcher.

ance methods to explore how to improve blood test communication
Objectives
1. Co-create an interpretation of study findings with the PPI panel and professional actors.
2. Bring together members of the public and primary care staff to develop strategies to improve blood test
communication.
3. Co-produce resources, including a workshop and public engagement materials, to help improve
communication about the limitations of blood tests.
Methods
Forum Theatre is a participatory performance method allowing participants to enact, critique and transform real-
world scenarios. It is one of the tools for social change described in Augusto Boal’s Theatre of the Oppressed,
developed in the 1960s/1970s with marginalised communities in Brazil.57
In a Forum Theatre workshop, actors perform a scene involving conflict or misunderstanding while audience
members (“spect-actors”) observe the social dynamics which emerge.58 A facilitator then invites spect-actors to
suggest or enact alternative actions to change the scene, improving the dramatised interaction through iterative
improvisation.58
Forum Theatre is gaining traction in research exploring how interpersonal interactions can be improved in
heal

Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
To develop this proposal, I held three workshops with 19 members of the public who had recently had general
practice blood tests. Participants were from diverse ethnic backgrounds, including Black British, South Asian and
Chinese, and ranged in age from 20s-70s. Several had long-term conditions and/or identified as disabled, and two
participants were neurodivergent.
In the first workshop I asked about participants’ experiences of blood testing, which helped shape my research
questions. In later workshops, we discussed the proposed research: the topic, aims and objectives, methods, and
plans for sharing findings. Participants felt strongly that this research was important. They highlighted
communication difficulties throughout the blood testing process:
 “I don’t feel the GP discusses the decision to do a blood test… they just say you need one.”
 “There’s a lot of difficulty in understanding the results without having to go onto Google.”
They also described concerns about blood test uncertainty or overuse:
 “It’s confusing getting a borderline result - the doctor says

gender and have a range of health experiences.
I will meet the panel three times a year to discuss study progress and troubleshoot any issues which come up. All
panel members will be paid for their time and travel expenses in line with NIHR guidance. I will also offer payments
for childcare/caring expenses. The research budget includes funding for panel members to access training and

Consent
I will seek consent for recording from patients and clinicians either prospectively (for face-to-face consultations) or
retrospectively (e.g. if seeking to access audio consultation data from practice telephone systems). Participants in
PPI workshops were surprised that many GP practices automatically recorded telephone consultations and felt
patients needed to be told this before being approached for consent. I will consider this in the consent procedure,
with input from the PPI panel and research sites. Clinicians and patients will be able to withdraw their consent for
recording during the consultation or request that the recorded data be destroyed at any point afterwards.
I discuss the feasibility of recording further in Section 9.

Aims
This research will use an ethnographic approach to explore how and whether blood test uncertainty, overuse and
overdiagnosis are discussed in primary care settings. It will employ participatory methods to identify how these
issues could be better communicated.
Research questions
1. How is communication about blood testing in primary care settings framed in the literature?
2. How, where and when do issues like uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis emerge in primary care
interactions about blood testing?
3. What challenges do clinicians face in communicating about blood tests, and how can video-reflexive
ethnography help clinicians to identify solutions to these challenges?
4. How can participatory performance methods engage patients and clinicians to identify more effective ways
of discussing issues with blood tests such as uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis?
Methods
A multi-method study across four work packages (WPs):
 WP1: Meta-ethnography synthesising existing qualitative literature on primary care blood test
communication.
 WP2: Detailed ethnographic observation in two general practices with contrasting patient populations,
focusing on consultat

ions where blood tests are discussed.
 WP3: Video-reflexive ethnography workshops with primary care staff to identify complex challenges
around blood testing communication as well as opportunities for change.
 WP4: Forum Theatre workshops with members of the public and primary care staff to develop strategies
for improving blood test communication. The findings will inform a co-produced workshop (for clinicians)
and an online resource (for patients) to support better blood testing discussions.
Impact
This participatory research project will highlight the primary care consultation as a key site of opportunity for
prudent blood testing. It will engage primary care staff and the public in co-producing resources to encourage more
effective communication about the limitations of blood tests.
Dissemination
The workshop resource will be shared with primary care staff and GP trainees locally, nationally and
internationally. A version of the workshop will be made available as a publicly-accessible resource for patients.
Research findings will be published in academic journals, presented at conferences, and shared through blogs and
podcasts.

s, and interactions.45,48,49
Sampling
Participants in PPI workshops felt it was important to explore blood testing practices in diverse patient groups and
across different geographical settings. I will therefore purposively select two contrasting GP practices: an inner-
city practice with a young, multi-ethnic population, and a suburban practice with an older population. I will use
General Practice Fingertips data to identify potential research sites which will be recruited with the support of the
NIHR Research Delivery Network.50
Data collection
I will undertake observation in each site 2-3 times per week over 4-6 months (approximately 200 hours in total).
This schedule, informed by similar studies,6,43,48 balances the need for deep immersion in the field with the
practical constraints of being a lone researcher.
Data collection will include:
1. Observation of professional practices
I will observe clinical consultations, reception/administrative areas, interprofessional meetings,
o
and Patient Participant Groups. Only half of primary care blood tests are ordered by GPs,5 so I
will also observe pharmacists, nurses and phlebotomists to ensure the dataset reflects the
diversity of pr

ance methods to explore how to improve blood test communication
Objectives
1. Co-create an interpretation of study findings with the PPI panel and professional actors.
2. Bring together members of the public and primary care staff to develop strategies to improve blood test
communication.
3. Co-produce resources, including a workshop and public engagement materials, to help improve
communication about the limitations of blood tests.
Methods
Forum Theatre is a participatory performance method allowing participants to enact, critique and transform real-
world scenarios. It is one of the tools for social change described in Augusto Boal’s Theatre of the Oppressed,
developed in the 1960s/1970s with marginalised communities in Brazil.57
In a Forum Theatre workshop, actors perform a scene involving conflict or misunderstanding while audience
members (“spect-actors”) observe the social dynamics which emerge.58 A facilitator then invites spect-actors to
suggest or enact alternative actions to change the scene, improving the dramatised interaction through iterative
improvisation.58
Forum Theatre is gaining traction in research exploring how interpersonal interactions can be improved in
heal

with input from the PPI panel and research sites. Clinicians and patients will be able to withdraw their consent for
recording during the consultation or request that the recorded data be destroyed at any point afterwards.
I discuss the feasibility of recording further in Section 9.

Research design
The project has four parts:
1. Evidence search: I will search and review existing research on how blood tests are discussed in general
practices. This will help to understand what is already known and what is missing.
2. Observation at GP practices: I will spend several months watching conversations about blood tests at two
different general practices. I will make recordings of patient consultations with GP staff (including GPs,
nurses, pharmacists and phlebotomists) to look at how blood tests are discussed.
3. Workshops with GP staff: I will show short recordings of blood test discussions to staff. They will consider
how blood tests are discussed with patients and think about how communication can be improved.
4. Theatre workshops with the public: Professional actors and public contributors will create a short play
based on the research. This will be performed to members of the public as part of “Forum Theatre”
workshops. Workshop attendees will be invited to make changes to the play to improve communication
about blood tests. Public contributors will help t

evidence.text:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
To develop this proposal, I held three workshops with 19 members of the public who had recently had general
practice blood tests. Participants were from diverse ethnic backgrounds, including Black British, South Asian and
Chinese, and ranged in age from 20s-70s. Several had long-term conditions and/or identified as disabled, and two
participants were neurodivergent.
In the first workshop I asked about participants’ experiences of blood testing, which helped shape my research
questions. In later workshops, we discussed the proposed research: the topic, aims and objectives, methods, and
plans for sharing findings. Participants felt strongly that this research was important. They highlighted
communication difficulties throughout the blood testing process:
 “I don’t feel the GP discusses the decision to do a blood test… they just say you need one.”
 “There’s a lot of difficulty in understanding the results without having to go onto Google.”
They also described concerns about blood test uncertainty or overuse:
 “It’s confusing getting a borderline result - the doctor says

b. An online education resource for patients.
Patient and Public Involvement (PPI)
People who have recently had blood tests will be involved throughout the project. Nineteen public contributors with
diverse ages, ethnic backgrounds, and health experiences helped to shape the project over three workshops.
They described many issues with communication about blood tests and felt strongly that this research was
important.
A PPI panel will guide the research to make sure the project responds to the needs and preferences of people
having blood tests. The panel will meet three times a year to review project plans at each stage and provide input
on data analysis. The panel will also write public summaries of the research.
Panel members will be paid for their contributions. They will receive funding for training and attending conferences
to share the research. Two lay people will co-chair a separate Project Advisory Group to steer the project.
Sharing the research

evidence.text:
nt urgent attention.
Aims
This research will use an ethnographic approach to explore how and whether blood test uncertainty, overuse and
overdiagnosis are discussed in primary care settings. It will employ participatory methods to identify how these
issues could be better communicated.
Research questions
1. How is communication about blood testing in primary care settings framed in the literature?
2. How, where and when do issues like uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis emerge in primary care
interactions about blood testing?
3. What challenges do clinicians face in communicating about blood tests, and how can video-reflexive
ethnography help clinicians to identify solutions to these challenges?
4. How can participatory performance methods engage patients and clinicians to identify more effective ways
of discussing issues with blood tests such as uncertainty, overuse and overdiagnosis?
Methods
A multi-method study across four work packages (WPs):
 WP1: Meta-ethnography synthesising existing qualitative literature on primary care blood test
communication.
 WP2: Detailed ethnographic observation in two general practices with contrasting patient populations,
focusing on consultat

ions where blood tests are discussed.
 WP3: Video-reflexive ethnography workshops with primary care staff to identify complex challenges
around blood testing communication as well as opportunities for change.
 WP4: Forum Theatre workshops with members of the public and primary care staff to develop strategies
for improving blood test communication. The findings will inform a co-produced workshop (for clinicians)
and an online resource (for patients) to support better blood testing discussions.
Impact
This participatory research project will highlight the primary care consultation as a key site of opportunity for
prudent blood testing. It will engage primary care staff and the public in co-producing resources to encourage more
effective communication about the limitations of blood tests.
Dissemination
The workshop resource will be shared with primary care staff and GP trainees locally, nationally and
internationally. A version of the workshop will be made available as a publicly-accessible resource for patients.
Research findings will be published in academic journals, presented at conferences, and shared through blogs and
podcasts.

s, and interactions.45,48,49
Sampling
Participants in PPI workshops felt it was important to explore blood testing practices in diverse patient groups and
across different geographical settings. I will therefore purposively select two contrasting GP practices: an inner-
city practice with a young, multi-ethnic population, and a suburban practice with an older population. I will use
General Practice Fingertips data to identify potential research sites which will be recruited with the support of the
NIHR Research Delivery Network.50
Data collection
I will undertake observation in each site 2-3 times per week over 4-6 months (approximately 200 hours in total).
This schedule, informed by similar studies,6,43,48 balances the need for deep immersion in the field with the
practical constraints of being a lone researcher.
Data collection will include:
1. Observation of professional practices
I will observe clinical consultations, reception/administrative areas, interprofessional meetings,
o
and Patient Participant Groups. Only half of primary care blood tests are ordered by GPs,5 so I
will also observe pharmacists, nurses and phlebotomists to ensure the dataset reflects the
diversity of pr

ance methods to explore how to improve blood test communication
Objectives
1. Co-create an interpretation of study findings with the PPI panel and professional actors.
2. Bring together members of the public and primary care staff to develop strategies to improve blood test
communication.
3. Co-produce resources, including a workshop and public engagement materials, to help improve
communication about the limitations of blood tests.
Methods
Forum Theatre is a participatory performance method allowing participants to enact, critique and transform real-
world scenarios. It is one of the tools for social change described in Augusto Boal’s Theatre of the Oppressed,
developed in the 1960s/1970s with marginalised communities in Brazil.57
In a Forum Theatre workshop, actors perform a scene involving conflict or misunderstanding while audience
members (“spect-actors”) observe the social dynamics which emerge.58 A facilitator then invites spect-actors to
suggest or enact alternative actions to change the scene, improving the dramatised interaction through iterative
improvisation.58
Forum Theatre is gaining traction in research exploring how interpersonal interactions can be improved in
heal

lso record consultations involving interpreters and patient advocates/carers. I have made provision in the
budget for patient information and consent forms to be translated into the three most common patient languages
(besides English) in each research site.
Consent
I will seek consent for recording from patients and clinicians either prospectively (for face-to-face consultations) or
retrospectively (e.g. if seeking to access audio consultation data from practice telephone systems). Participants in
PPI workshops were surprised that many GP practices automatically recorded telephone consultations and felt
patients needed to be told this before being approached for consent. I will consider this in the consent procedure,
with input from the PPI panel and research sites. Clinicians and patients will be able to withdraw their consent for
recording during the consultation or request that the recorded data be destroyed at any point afterwards.
I discuss the feasibility of recording further in Section 9.

Applicant CV: Shows increasing responsibility through leadership roles (Policy Director, Co-Chair) and recognition via teaching and leadership awards.
Applicant CV: Demonstrates a practitioner-academic career with clinical roles (GP Registrar, Locum Doctor) alongside research positions and clinical research awards.
Applicant Research Background: Clearly articulates a research vision focused on medical overuse and a career trajectory aiming for a Professor of Primary Care role.
Applicant Research Background: States the fellowship will develop complex qualitative methodologies and strengthen the portfolio, explaining why this award is the right next step.
Applicant Research Background: Details extensive experience in qualitative methods (ethnography, interviews) and formal training (MPhil, Diploma, PGCert), aligning with project needs.
